parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values on red and blue chips, with green chips known to be worth $0.50 to everyone. Buffalo values blue highly ($0.70) and red very little ($0.10). Bear values blue the most ($1.00) and red moderately ($0.60). Goose values both red and blue very little ($0.10 each). This creates clear gains from trade: Bear strongly wants blue, and Buffalo somewhat wants blue more than red; Goose is almost indifferent across red and blue but still likes green. Green acts as a stable currency everyone values equally. Because players only see their own values, they infer others’ valuations from who tries to buy or sell which color and at what ratios. Buffalo is naturally inclined to sell red and possibly green to acquire blue. Bear strongly tries to acquire blue and is relatively reluctant to give it up. Goose, with low values on red and blue but normal value on green, is a natural seller of red/blue in exchange for green, especially if others pay more than $0.10-per-chip effective value. Strategic uncertainty arises because acceptance is simultaneous and multiple acceptances are randomly resolved, so proposers must consider when an offer is attractive to more than one counterparty. Early rounds are likely exploratory, with conservative trades involving green as a safe baseline; by later rounds, the trade history reveals rough preferences (e.g., who repeatedly tries to buy blue), enabling more targeted surplus-enhancing trades. However, the proposers still never know exact private values, so they shade offers in their favor and sometimes get declined when they misjudge how much surplus they are leaving the counterparties. Overall, we should see Buffalo and Bear competing for blue and using green as payment, while Goose opportunistically sells red/blue to gain green with small but positive personal surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players start with symmetric chip holdings but different private values. No one yet has information about others’ preferences beyond the common knowledge about green. We expect cautious, surplus-positive trades that use green as a medium of exchange and small quantities to test others’ willingness to trade. Buffalo is likely to attempt to trade away low-value red for something potentially more valuable like blue or green. Bear may already start trying to buy blue if it can do so without giving up too much green. Goose should be willing to part with some red or blue if compensated with green, but at the start may not know where the best counterparties are, so may try simple, balanced-looking trades.

By round 1, the one successful trade in round 0 has slightly revealed preferences: Bear was willing to give up two green chips to gain one blue, and Goose accepted that, indicating Goose does not value blue highly relative to green. Buffalo observed this and can infer that both Bear and Goose at least somewhat prefer blue differently than green. Holdings are now: Buffalo unchanged (10R,10G,10B), Bear (10R,8G,11B), Goose (10R,12G,9B). Bear’s stockpile of blue suggests Bear will be more reluctant to sell blue and more protective of remaining green. Goose, now rich in green, may be content but still open to slightly favorable trades. Buffalo still has strong motivation to transform low-value red into blue or possibly green, and may adjust offers to be more attractive after seeing the first declinations.

Going into round 2, holdings reflect two accepted trades. After Buffalo’s successful purchase of blue from Goose in round 1, the inventories are: Buffalo (10R,7G,12B), Bear (10R,8G,11B), Goose (10R,14G,7B). Buffalo has increased blue holdings and reduced green, Bear is blue-rich and slightly green-poor, and Goose is very green-rich but blue-poor. This pattern reveals that Bear and Buffalo both place meaningful value on blue, while Goose is willing to part with it for green. Everyone knows that green is safe value at $0.50, but the exact strength of others’ blue preferences is still uncertain. With only one round left, players may try slightly more aggressive trades that still guarantee them non-negative surplus, often using green to rebalance portfolios. Buffalo may try to pick up a bit more blue or sell some red; Bear might seek to replenish green or marginally adjust its red/blue mix; Goose, sitting on extra green, may try for opportunistic but safe gains by offering red or blue for modest amounts of green.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this three-player chip bargaining game, each player begins with 10 chips of each color (RED, BLUE, GREEN) and privately values them slightly differently, except for GREEN which is common-value at $0.50 for all. RED is worth $1.00 to everyone, but BLUE ranges from $0.40 (otter) to $0.50 (peacock) to $0.70 (cat). Everyone publicly sees that each color is somewhere within a common range, so they know that RED is likely at the high end of the range, GREEN is fixed at $0.50, and BLUE could be more or less valuable depending on the player. The main source of gains from trade comes from BLUE: cat likely values BLUE more than others, otter the least, with peacock in between. All players know only their own exact values, but they infer that others might differ, particularly on BLUE, creating strategic uncertainty about how aggressive or generous to be. Because RED is clearly strong and symmetric, players expect RED to be hard to acquire and unlikely to be traded away cheaply; GREEN is neutral and can serve as a medium of exchange when values are ambiguous. Given three players and random selection when multiple parties accept, proposers have to structure offers that will induce exactly one likely accepter or at least be indifferent about which counterparty they get. The surplus calculator makes it straightforward for each player to see whether each proposed trade is good or bad for them personally at the moment of decision. As a result, accepted trades are almost always individually profitable in value terms, but may be myopic: players might accept small positive gains now even if that hampers better deals later. Otter, having low BLUE value, is naturally inclined to trade away BLUE and hoard RED; cat wants to acquire BLUE and is relatively more willing to give up GREEN or even some RED if the margin is attractive. Peacock is in between and might opportunistically play both sides: sell some BLUE to cat when cat overpays, or pick up BLUE cheaply from otter if possible. Early rounds likely feature exploratory offers that test how aggressively others will trade and whether anyone seems desperate for particular colors. As the game proceeds, some players will accumulate more of their high-value chips, while others may miss out if they hold out for unrealistically favorable trades. The fixed three-round, three-turn structure also pushes some players—especially those who haven’t yet secured a good trade—toward accepting modest, safe gains in later turns rather than risking ending the game with the initial endowment only. Because each player can see the evolving holdings, proposers in later rounds can tailor offers to exploit inventory imbalances (for example, targeting a cat that already has many BLUE chips and might still accept more if not too expensive). Overall, we expect a few small, clearly positive-sum trades, somewhat conservative use of RED (since all know it is highly valuable), and at least one substantial BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN transfer that favors cat in final surplus, while otter monetizes BLUE into safer RED/Green and peacock tries to extract small gains from both sides without overcommitting.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has yet seen any behavior from others, so beliefs about others’ valuations are based only on the public ranges and general intuition (e.g., that BLUE may vary and RED is very strong). Players are likely to start with cautious, mildly favorable offers that test demand and reveal little information. Otter, moving first, is especially uncertain and may try to structure a trade that offloads some low-valued BLUE or neutral GREEN in exchange for more RED, but at terms that are still positive for whoever accepts. Peacock, moving second, will have the benefit of observing otter’s opening move and can pitch a somewhat more targeted offer, perhaps trying to sell medium-value BLUE or GREEN to pick up extra RED. Cat, moving last, will respond after observing two full proposals and may decide whether to act more aggressively to acquire BLUE (which cat privately values most), or to seize any mispriced opportunities involving GREEN. Rejections are relatively likely in this opening round as players feel out the space and avoid being exploited by offers that seem tilted against them.

By the start of round 1, some information has effectively leaked through the pattern of acceptances. Otter has willingly traded away BLUE for RED and then GREEN for BLUE, signaling that otter is relatively indifferent between BLUE and GREEN but values RED strongly. Peacock traded away GREEN for RED and had an early offer rejected, suggesting that peacock also rates RED highly but is not eager to move BLUE just yet. Cat successfully bought BLUE with GREEN from otter, slightly reinforcing the possibility that cat values BLUE more. With these signals, the second-round dynamics shift: otter may continue to churn low-valued BLUE into higher-valued RED, but now also has more BLUE holdings after the previous trade. Peacock, seeing that otter and cat both engaged in trades, may seek a more clearly profitable move, perhaps trying to arbitrage GREEN or BLUE. Cat, confident in a high BLUE valuation, might start to test whether others will part with additional BLUE in exchange for GREEN, or even limited amounts of RED. However, players are still cautious; no one wants to overpay in RED, so GREEN is likely to be the main payment token in this round, with BLUE being shifted to whoever privately values it most.

Entering the final round, the remaining opportunities for gain are limited and inventory imbalances are more visible. Cat has accumulated extra BLUE via trades with both otter and peacock, confirming to others that cat values BLUE enough to give up both GREEN and some RED for it. Otter has been the main counterparty in BLUE-for-GREEN trades and has also acquired some additional RED from peacock, reinforcing otter’s strategy of converting BLUE into safer value. Peacock has managed a favorable BLUE-for-RED swap with cat but has otherwise been more cautious, declining some offers and seeing others rejected. Because this is the last round, players can no longer hold out for future gains: a small positive surplus now is better than zero later. At the same time, no one wants to destroy value by trading away RED unless the margin is clear. Proposers in this final sequence therefore tend to present relatively straightforward, mildly generous offers that target their comparative advantages: peacock may seek to offload remaining GREEN for either RED or BLUE; otter may try one last time to trade away excess BLUE for RED; cat, realizing this is the last chance to exploit a high BLUE valuation, might offer GREEN to pull in any remaining BLUE that others are still holding. Final accepted trades in this round tend to be modest, closing off residual gains without radically changing any one player’s portfolio.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features symmetric endowments (10 of each chip color) but asymmetric private values on red and blue chips. Green is common-value at $0.50 each for everyone, so it acts as a natural numeraire and "safe" medium of exchange. Badger values both red and blue highly at $0.80; Camel has a moderate value for red ($0.60) and low for blue ($0.40); Squid has low value for red ($0.20) and moderate for blue ($0.60). This creates clear comparative advantages: Badger is generally the high buyer of both red and blue, Squid is a natural seller of red and potential buyer/seller of blue, and Camel is an intermediate trader, especially inclined to buy red and sell blue. However, players only know their own values and must infer others' preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. The UI surplus calculator ensures that no one will knowingly accept a trade that is negative in their own private value, so accepted trades will typically increase both parties' payoffs (or at least be non-negative). The main bargaining tension comes from uncertainty about how much others value each chip and competition to be selected when multiple players are willing to accept a trade. Proposers must choose offers that are beneficial to themselves, yet attractive enough that at least one other player strictly benefits, while anticipating that they cannot target a specific partner if both potential recipients accept. Early in the game, proposals tend to be exploratory and modest, using green chips to probe counterpart valuations. As history accumulates, players update beliefs about others' preferences, fine-tuning offers to exploit known comparative advantages (e.g., Badger trying to acquire under-valued reds from Squid, or sell blues to Camel). Because there are only three rounds and each player proposes once per round, there is limited time to converge to fully efficient allocations, so many trades will be simple, low-risk exchanges rather than finely optimized tariffs. Overall, we expect small but mutually beneficial trades in which badger gradually accumulates both red and blue from the others, Camel shifts toward more red and fewer blue, and Squid offloads red and perhaps trades blue or green depending on inferred prices, with some proposals being declined when they fail to offer clear surplus to at least one counterpart.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game all players have symmetric holdings and no information about others' valuations beyond the public value ranges. Round 0 proposals are likely to be exploratory, small in size, and often use green chips as a neutral yardstick. Badger, valuing both red and blue highly, may try to cheaply acquire either color using green or by offering one high-valued color for another in a ratio that still benefits them. Camel might experiment with trades that increase red at the expense of blue or green, while Squid will be inclined to sell red (which they value very low) in exchange for blue or green. Because no player yet has clear signals about others' preferences, some offers will be declined for being unappealing or too asymmetric from the recipients' perspectives.

By Round 1, the first two accepted trades from Round 0 reveal useful information. Badger's willingness to pay 1.5 green per red signals that red is quite valuable to them. Squid's acceptance of that trade, plus their willingness to trade red for blue with Camel, hints that Squid's valuation of red is low relative to both green and blue. Camel's trade of blue for red suggests red is more valuable than blue for Camel. Given these signals, players can update beliefs: Badger is likely a strong buyer of red, Squid a low-value red holder, and Camel a moderate red buyer who is happy to offload some blue. This creates a clearer pattern of comparative advantage: Squid can profit by continuing to sell red, Camel by balancing its blue and red holdings, and Badger by buying red (and possibly blue) when the price is favorable. Proposals now tend to be more targeted, with ratios near indifference points inferred from previous accepted offers, while still being modest in size to avoid overpaying amid lingering uncertainty.

Entering Round 2, substantial information has accumulated: everyone has seen consistent patterns of Squid selling red, Camel shifting toward more red and fewer blue, and Badger repeatedly seeking red (and sometimes blue) when prices are favorable. Holdings are now more differentiated, and remaining opportunities for mutually beneficial trade are smaller but still present, especially involving red redistribution from Squid to the others and potential fine-tuning of blue holdings between Badger and Camel. Because this is the final round, there is no value in strategic delay. Proposers will attempt any clearly positive-sum trades that remain, often modest in scale, while avoiding offers that might accidentally give too generous a surplus to a counterpart. Declines can still occur if a proposal is unbalanced for the respondent or if earlier trades have already largely exhausted the obvious gains from trade for certain color pairs.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game revolves around trading red and blue chips, with green chips having the same value to everyone. Each player begins with 10 of each color and private valuations for red and blue, while everyone knows that green is always worth $0.50. Dog values red very highly ($1.00) and blue very little ($0.20). Butterfly values blue highly ($0.90) and red moderately ($0.60). Ram values both red and blue quite low ($0.40 and $0.10 respectively). Because all players know only their own valuations, they must infer others’ values from observed offers and acceptances. Strategic tension stems from three forces: (1) mutual gains from trade when a chip moves toward someone who values it more; (2) the proposer’s temptation to demand most of the surplus; and (3) the uncertainty about others’ valuations and willingness to accept, especially early in the game. Dog’s natural pattern is to try to convert blue into red, sacrificing low-value blue for high-value red; Butterfly wants the opposite, converting red into blue; Ram, with low values on both red and blue, will typically try to offload red and blue in return for green or accept deals that raise green holdings, unless the rate is clearly unfavorable. The random selection when multiple players accept makes it risky to post highly attractive deals that more than one counterpart would like; proposers must anticipate not just whether a proposal is accepted but by whom. Over three rounds, as small deals reveal approximate relative values (e.g., how much red someone is willing to give for blue), players can refine inferences, but the limited horizon and short sequence of turns curbs elaborate signaling. Expected dynamics are that early proposals are exploratory and moderately generous to elicit information, and later proposals adjust tighter toward each proposer’s preferred direction once they see which counterpart is more likely to accept particular chip directions and ratios. Green chips, being common-value, serve as a focal medium for Ram (who is poor in private values) to cash out of low-value colored chips and for others to gauge how desperate Ram is to move out of red or blue. Across the three rounds forecast below, we model plausible, self-interested play where each proposal represents a locally attractive move from the sender’s perspective, while acceptances are consistent with each responder’s private surplus calculations and evolving beliefs about others’ values.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, everyone is uninformed about others’ exact valuations and is likely to experiment with conservative, slightly generous trades that are clearly profitable to themselves but not so extreme that they are obviously unfair. Dog is motivated to pick up red and shed blue; Butterfly wants to shed red for blue; Ram is inclined to grab green when possible or accept deals that slightly improve their total value. Proposals in this round will tend to involve small quantities, both to limit downside risk and to probe who is willing to trade what for what. Rejections here mainly indicate that the terms are too one-sided, not yet that trading directions are wrong. A first accepted trade, if it occurs, provides important information about at least one partner’s relative valuations.

By round 1, players have learned something from round 0. Dog has successfully traded away blue for red with Butterfly, so Dog infers Butterfly is willing to part with red and likes blue. Butterfly, having accepted the earlier trade and Ram’s red-for-green trade, sees Ram is eager to move red into green and that Dog values red more than blue. Ram has now revealed a willingness to give up red cheaply for green and has observed that Butterfly is ready to exploit favorable terms in that direction. With this partial information, proposals become slightly more targeted: Dog tries to push again on red-for-blue, Butterfly explores getting more blue while preserving some surplus, and Ram seeks another conversion of low-value colored chips into green. Some proposals fail when they push too far on the exchange rate, but the directions of trade are now clearer to everyone.

Round 2 is the final opportunity to trade, so players become more willing to lock in modest gains rather than hold out for ideal terms. Dog has profited twice by swapping blue for red with Butterfly and may attempt one last, slightly less generous variant; Butterfly, understanding that blue is valuable and time is short, may only accept if still clearly profitable. Ram’s exploratory attempt to sell blue for green was rejected in round 1, signaling that others see blue as relatively valuable; Ram might now focus on a safer, small red-for-green exchange, which Butterfly is likely to accept if it remains favorable. The interplay of these incentives produces one more Dog–Butterfly trade and one last Ram–Butterfly conversion, with middle proposal(s) that push too close to the margin being turned down as players guard against overpaying in the final moves.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with symmetric endowments (10 of each chip), but asymmetric valuations over red and blue chips, with green as a common-value chip worth $0.50 to everyone. Iguana values red the most (RED $0.80, BLUE $0.40), lion values blue the most (BLUE $1.00, RED $0.40), and alligator is relatively blue-oriented but with weaker spreads (BLUE $0.60, RED $0.30). Efficient trade moves red chips toward iguana and blue chips toward lion, using green as a safe, neutral medium of exchange when needed. However, players only see their own private values, so they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. Early in the game, offers are likely small and conservative, testing who likes which colors. Because each player has a surplus calculator, they will almost never accept a trade that yields negative surplus, and they will be indifferent (and often reluctant) about tiny gains; proposals that are clearly positive for both sides are most likely to be accepted. Random assignment of a partner when multiple players accept creates a risk that a proposer who makes too generous an offer could end up trading with the less favorable acceptor, so proposers tend to structure trades that are good but not excessively generous to any responder. Over three rounds, we expect gradual convergence toward value-increasing exchanges: red drifting to iguana, blue drifting to lion, and green sometimes being moved as a neutral sweetener. Still, incomplete information and limited time mean the outcome will not be fully efficient, and some mutually beneficial trades will remain unrealized.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has observed any behavior yet, so each proposer is essentially probing the market. They know only their own valuations and the public fact that others’ red and blue values lie within $0.10–$1.00. Rational players will prefer modest, nearly risk-free trades that appear likely to be beneficial for at least one counterpart, while avoiding large, speculative offers. Iguana and lion have strong, opposite preferences over red and blue, but they have not yet discovered this through interaction. Green, being common-value, is the safest medium of exchange. This round primarily serves as information-gathering, with small, exploratory proposals that start to reveal who likes which chip colors.

By round 1, a bit of information has been revealed. From lion’s accepted trade with alligator in round 0, both of them see that exchanging green for blue at a 2:1 rate was appealing to alligator (who gained $0.20) but unappealing to iguana (who would have lost $0.20). This suggests that alligator values blue more than iguana does. Lion also infers that blue is reasonably valuable to at least one counterpart, while green is a flexible bargaining chip. Iguana’s failed attempt to buy red with green at a 1:1 rate shows that neither alligator nor lion is eager to give up red cheaply, hinting that red might be moderately valuable to them. In this round, proposals become slightly more targeted: alligator continues to pursue blue, lion experiments with red–blue exchanges in a way that might attract iguana, and iguana becomes somewhat more cautious after the initial rejection.

Going into the final round, some important structure is clearer. Iguana and lion have just executed a red-for-blue trade, from which lion infers that iguana strongly prefers red over blue and iguana infers that lion strongly prefers blue over red. Alligator has repeatedly shown a willingness to trade green or red in pursuit of blue, while both others have been selective about giving up red. Because time is almost over and remaining turns are limited, players may push slightly harder for advantageous trades, but each still avoids negative-surplus deals. We expect final attempts to move red further toward iguana and blue toward lion or alligator, with green possibly used to sweeten terms. Nonetheless, residual uncertainty about exact valuations and the risk of overpaying lead to some attractive but not fully exploited opportunities, so a few last offers are declined despite positive potential surplus from a full-information perspective.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common known value for green chips. Each player starts with an equal, symmetric endowment (10 of each color), but their valuations differ: whale-red-86 values red most (1.00) and blue least (0.40); dolphin-orange-3122 values blue relatively high (0.80) and red lower (0.60); duck-orange-4971 values red highly (1.00) and blue moderately (0.60). Green is worth 0.50 to everyone. This structure creates multiple potential surplus-creating trades, especially exchanging red for blue between dolphin and the two red-lovers, and exchanging blue for red from whale or duck to dolphin. However, each player only sees their own values during play and must infer others' preferences from the pattern of proposals and acceptances. With three rounds and one turn per player per round, there are nine total opportunities for trade, with chip holdings updating after each executed trade. Incentives: each proposer wants to offer trades that are clearly profitable for themselves while leaving at least non-negative, preferably positive, surplus for others to increase acceptance chances. But because any accepting responder could be randomly chosen when multiple accept, proposals that are attractive to more than one responder create uncertainty over who will actually trade, which can matter when a proposer might prefer to shift specific colors toward or away from particular opponents. Non-senders decide whether to accept purely based on their own surplus calculator: any strictly value-increasing trade is likely to be accepted unless they speculate about better future opportunities or strategic signaling. Early in the game, players have little information about others' valuations, so opening offers may be exploratory—testing which colors others are willing to give up cheaply. As the game progresses, prior offers and responses can hint, for example, that someone values red highly if they frequently try to buy red or refuse to sell red except at a premium. Given the symmetric endowment and short horizon (only three rounds), the most straightforward behavior is near-myopic: accept any trade with non-negative surplus and propose high-margin trades that might still be acceptable if opponents value colors differently. In this forecast, players plausibly discover that dolphin is willing to pay more for blue and less for red, while whale and duck are both strong red holders. This leads to a sequence where dolphin tends to sell red and green to acquire blue, whale frequently tries to convert blue and green into red, and duck selectively participates in trades that boost red or green at acceptable terms. Not all offers clear the market; some are too one-sided given the responders’ private values, or fail because the specific responder who would have accepted cannot do so profitably. By the final round, players have partial but imperfect information about others’ preferences, leading to somewhat more targeted but still uncertain proposals before the game ends.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have symmetric holdings (10 of each color) and no information about others’ valuations beyond public ranges. Proposals here are likely to be exploratory, with each sender testing whether others are willing to part with colors they themselves value less. Whale-red-86, who privately values red highest, is inclined to try to buy red or sell blue; dolphin-orange-3122, who values blue highly and red less, will tend to sell red or green to get blue; duck-orange-4971 also values red highly and blue moderately, and may tentatively seek red or protect it. With no prior trade history, proposals that are too aggressive (leaving little or no surplus to others) are likely to be declined.

Going into round 1, some information has been revealed through accepted trades. Dolphin accepted whale’s trade supplying 3 BLUE for 2 RED, which is profitable for both and hints to each that the other is willing to trade between red and blue at roughly that rate. Dolphin also bought BLUE from duck with GREEN, again at favorable terms for both, suggesting to duck that dolphin places decent value on BLUE, while duck is relatively indifferent between BLUE and GREEN. Whale now holds more RED and less BLUE, confirming their red-seeking behavior to others; dolphin holds fewer RED, more BLUE, and fewer GREEN; duck holds more GREEN, fewer BLUE, and the same RED. Proposers in this round can update: whale may continue trying to acquire RED from others; dolphin may try to further convert RED or GREEN into BLUE; duck, having seen that BLUE can be sold profitably, may look for either additional GREEN or higher-value RED but must avoid trades that are too costly given their moderate value for BLUE.

By round 2, the trading pattern is clearer. Whale repeatedly trades to gain RED at the expense of BLUE or GREEN and has accepted poor terms when forced to give up RED, signaling a strong valuation for RED and lower for BLUE and GREEN. Dolphin consistently seeks BLUE, selling RED and GREEN; this behavior indicates dolphin’s relatively higher value for BLUE. Duck appears more flexible, buying RED with GREEN when the rate is favorable and selling BLUE for GREEN or selling RED only when compensated sufficiently. With only one proposal left for each player and no future bargaining, decisions are largely myopic: each sender tries to squeeze out any remaining profitable trades at the margin, and responders accept any strictly value-increasing offers without worrying about long-term positioning. However, current chip inventories constrain what can be offered; some potentially profitable trades do not occur simply because the required chip balances or mutually acceptable terms cannot be aligned within the final three turns.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features two privately valued chip colors (RED, BLUE) and one common-value chip (GREEN at $0.50 each for everyone). Each player starts with 10 of each color, so the only way to improve is to trade away chips that are relatively low value for them in exchange for chips that are relatively high value, given their own private valuations. The key asymmetries are: turtle values BLUE highest ($1.00) and RED slightly less ($0.90); gorilla values RED highest ($1.00) and BLUE less ($0.70); zebra values both RED and BLUE weakly ($0.60 and $0.50). This creates a natural bilateral efficiency pattern: turtle wants to buy BLUE and is relatively okay selling RED; gorilla wants to buy RED and is somewhat okay selling BLUE; zebra is comparatively weak on both RED and BLUE and thus has less to gain from trading those, but might sell BLUE to turtle or RED to gorilla if offered a good enough price. GREEN is neutral and equally valued by all; trading GREEN generally just reallocates common-value surplus and will mostly be used as a flexible medium or sweetener when someone wants to induce an acceptance or conceal their private valuation structure. However, because the posted calculator shows exact private net gains or losses, players will not intentionally take negative-value trades. The main strategic uncertainty is about the others’ valuations and reservation rates: nobody knows that turtle loves BLUE and gorilla loves RED, but they may infer it from repeated attempts to buy those colors or from the generosity (or stinginess) of offers. Since acceptances are simultaneous and, if multiple accept, the recipient is chosen randomly, proposers do not get to pick their partner directly; they must design offers that are attractive to at least one counterpart but not so generous that both are drawn in when that randomness is undesirable. Over three rounds (nine total proposals), players are likely to start with exploratory and mildly profitable offers, learn from what is accepted or declined, and then concentrate on high-surplus trades: turtle trying to accumulate BLUE, gorilla trying to accumulate RED, and zebra occasionally monetizing her relatively low valuations if given favorable terms. Declines will be common when offers are not obviously positive for recipients, and many accepted trades will be simple one-color-for-one-color or slightly asymmetric bundles that both sides see as a clear net gain given their private values.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, nobody has information about others’ private valuations. Each proposer is likely to start with simple, exploratory offers that are mildly favorable to themselves according to their surplus calculator, designed to test whether anyone is eager to trade a particular color. The main dynamic is information revelation: which chip colors people try to buy or sell, and how generous they are, will start to signal where their priorities lie. Because the acceptance rule is simultaneous and random if multiple accept, proposers will likely structure offers that are plausibly attractive to at least one counterpart, but not so generous that they are guaranteed to be oversubscribed.

By the second round, the trade history provides some weak signals: gorilla has shown a willingness to sell BLUE for RED and zebra has revealed a threshold for how she values RED relative to GREEN. Turtle’s initial RED-for-BLUE offer was not attractive enough to either counterpart, hinting that others may not be desperate for RED at even terms. Proposers now start to adjust: turtle can infer that simply swapping RED for BLUE one-for-one will not entice trade and may try improving prices or using GREEN as a sweetener. Gorilla knows that at least zebra is responsive to BLUE-for-RED terms and may refine those. Zebra, having had one profitable trade, may test whether either counterpart will overpay for her relatively low-value colors, though she remains cautious to avoid apparent losses.

Going into the final round, players have seen which chip colors each counterpart is willing to move and at roughly what implicit prices. Turtle has begun accumulating BLUE via trades that gorilla found acceptable, and gorilla has successfully increased RED holdings, often at zebra’s expense in terms of color composition but not in terms of her private value. Zebra has revealed willingness to trade both RED and GREEN when her calculator shows clear gains. With only three proposal turns left, each proposer now focuses on any remaining easy surplus: turtle tries for one more favorable BLUE acquisition, gorilla seeks a last RED-focused improvement, and zebra looks for a safe, value-adding swap or declines risky offers. Because there is no future beyond this round, there is no reason to hold back surplus; players will accept any strictly positive trade that appears and will decline anything non-positive.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player bargaining game with incomplete information about others’ red and blue chip values but common knowledge that green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone. Each player starts with 10 of each color, and only knows their own dollar values: cat (RED=0.90, BLUE=0.60), iguana (RED=0.80, BLUE=0.30), zebra (RED=0.40, BLUE=1.00). This creates predictable comparative advantages: cat and iguana both value red more than zebra does, while zebra values blue much more than the others. That structure makes red-for-blue trades especially promising: cat and iguana should want to buy blue and sell red; zebra should want to buy red and sell blue. Green is neutral and common-value, so it is mainly a balancing chip rather than a source of pure gains from trade. However, players do not know each other’s exact values. Early proposals are likely to be simple, ‘safe-looking’ trades that give the proposer a positive surplus according to their own calculator but are not obviously terrible offers to others. Because any accepting responder can be randomly chosen as the partner, proposers have incentives to craft deals that could plausibly be attractive to multiple recipients, but responders will only accept if their own calculator shows a gain. As history unfolds, observed acceptances and rejections provide weak signals about others’ valuations: e.g., zebra willingly trading away blue or demanding a lot of red reveals information. Given only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, players face tension between experimenting to learn about others’ types and exploiting the best currently believed surplus opportunities. We should expect at least a few red–blue trades that move chips toward those who value them most (zebra getting red, cat & iguana getting blue), possibly with some misfires where offers are declined because they are too one-sided or misaligned with others’ unknown values.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has symmetric endowments and only private knowledge of their own dollar values. Players know green is common-value and not a big source of private gains, so the main uncertainty is about red and blue valuations. Round 0 proposals are likely to be exploratory, relatively small trades that test the market without giving away too much surplus. Because there is no prior trade history, senders do not yet know who especially wants red or blue, so they may make offers that look reasonable under many possible opponent types. Responders, however, will only accept if their calculators show a clear gain, so any strongly one-sided trial offers are likely declined. Information from who accepts what (and at what terms) begins to shape expectations for later rounds.

After Round 0, some structure has emerged. Iguana and zebra have just completed mutually profitable red–blue trades: iguana traded away red for blue, and zebra traded away blue for red with iguana. This suggests, from cat’s perspective, that iguana probably values blue more relative to red than zebra does, and that zebra is at least willing to part with blue for red at some terms. Iguana learns that zebra will accept moderate blue-for-red ratios, and zebra sees that iguana is an eager trading partner for cross-color deals. In Round 1, players are more willing to propose slightly larger or more targeted trades, still focusing on moving red toward zebra and blue toward cat and iguana. However, they remain cautious about overpaying because valuations are still not perfectly known, and the randomness in partner selection if multiple accept means offers still must look attractive to more than one possible counterparty when feasible.

Going into the final round, substantial red–blue reallocation has already occurred, especially between iguana and zebra, with cat having managed to acquire some extra blue as well. Everyone now has a clearer picture that zebra is willing to pay red for blue and that iguana is consistently happy to trade red for blue at favorable ratios. Cat, observing that most beneficial cross-color gains have been partially exploited and that both counterparts now have more skewed portfolios, faces more limited and asymmetric opportunities. Players’ incentives now tilt toward squeezing out any remaining small surplus trades without risking value-destroying moves, since there are no future rounds to ‘correct’ mistakes. This typically yields either small, conservative proposals or no-agreement outcomes when proposed ratios no longer generate positive surplus for both sides given the now-shifted holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known valuations over red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. All players start with symmetric endowments (10 of each color), but their per-chip values differ: bear-purple-7271 values red and blue equally at 0.80, peacock-red-7768 values red at 0.80 and blue at 0.70, and swan-purple-1670 values blue at 0.80 and red at 0.50. Green chips are 0.50 for all. This creates cross-player gains from trade: red is relatively more valuable to bear and peacock than to swan; blue is relatively more valuable to bear and swan than to peacock. Efficient trades will tend to send red from swan to bear/peacock and blue from peacock to bear/swan, potentially using green as a low-stakes medium of exchange or as a way to make small side payments. However, each player only knows their own valuations and must infer others' preferences from observed offers and acceptances, while also recognizing that if both non-senders accept an offer, a recipient is chosen at random. Because there are only three rounds and each player proposes once per round, the game is short, and players face tension between experimenting to learn (revealing information about their preferences) and exploiting likely profitable trades quickly. The surplus calculator means no one will knowingly accept a negative-value trade, so feasible agreements must be strictly beneficial for the responder and at least weakly beneficial for the proposer. Strategic uncertainty arises because proposers cannot target a specific partner when both could profitably accept, so they may design offers that are attractive primarily to one counterpart. Over the game, we should expect a few small, clearly positive-sum trades that move chips from relatively low-value holders to higher-value holders, especially in red and blue, while green typically moves only when needed to fine-tune surplus splits or signal willingness to trade.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players have symmetric chip holdings and only know their own values. They will likely use early proposals to probe others' willingness to trade red for blue and vice versa, and to experiment with green as a low-risk sweetener. Because no one has yet revealed preferences, initial offers are expected to be conservative, producing modest but positive gains for both sides and avoiding trades that could accidentally be highly favorable to the partner. Players also begin to learn who seems more eager to part with particular colors based on acceptance patterns.

By round 1, some information has been revealed. The declined green-for-blue offers from bear and peacock suggest that both counterparts value blue relatively highly compared to green, making cheap blue acquisitions infeasible. Swan's accepted trade to buy red with green indicates that at least one of the others was willing to give up red at close to its value, hinting that red is not extremely scarce in their preferences. Now, proposers are more likely to attempt direct red–blue swaps that create clear cross-player gains, using smaller quantities and more balanced terms to ensure mutual benefit while still being attractive enough to elicit acceptance.

In the final round, chip holdings have shifted: red has tended to flow toward bear and peacock, and blue toward bear and swan, with green used selectively. Players now have a better sense of others' relative values and are under time pressure to finalize any remaining profitable trades. With only one proposal each left, they may attempt slightly more aggressive but still mutually beneficial offers that consolidate their portfolios toward higher-valued colors, particularly using green as a residual instrument to balance marginal benefits without taking large risks.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric endowments (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but have different private values for red and blue chips. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so green acts like money: each green chip is a frictionless $0.50 token all players know is equally useful. The private values create tensions over which colors are most desirable: camel and monkey are both relatively red-loving and strongly blue-loving compared to gorilla, while gorilla values both red and blue only $0.30. Monkey in particular values blue very highly ($0.90) but red weakly ($0.20), making blue-for-red trades with the others attractive if the terms are favorable. However, no one knows the others’ exact valuations; they only see the surplus calculator for their own side. This creates uncertainty and strategic experimentation. Proposers must pick one color to buy and one to sell, with positive quantities of each, never offering more than they hold. Non-senders accept if and only if a proposal shows non-negative surplus for them; losing-money trades are extremely unlikely to be accepted. Because multiple acceptances resolve randomly, proposers cannot target a specific partner with a favorable deal; instead, they craft terms that are acceptable to at least one counterpart, sometimes both. As the game proceeds, accepted offers reveal partial information about others’ preferences (e.g., willingness to give up certain colors at specific rates), influencing later proposals. With only three rounds of three proposals each, players face pressure to secure clear, calculator-confirmed gains quickly rather than hold out for theoretically perfect trades. Likely dynamics include early conservative offers that give small but safe gains to the sender, mid-game refinements as players infer who values which color, and late-game attempts to grab high-value chips (especially BLUE) with still-positive but sometimes thinner margins for the counterpart. Because green is valued equally, many trades that move only green for a single color at equal or worse dollar value to the recipient will be rejected; attractive deals usually involve trading away lower-valued chips (for that player) in exchange for higher-valued ones, with the proposer leaving just enough surplus on the table that at least one counterpart sees a non-negative gain.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players are symmetric in holdings but asymmetric in valuations, and no one yet has evidence about others’ private values. Proposers in round 0 are likely to make conservative, low-quantity offers that ensure a clear personal gain according to their calculators while testing whether counterparts will trade away particular colors. Gorilla moves first with no information and will probably try to slightly upgrade into the color they suspect might be undervalued by others, leaving enough potential surplus that someone might accept. Camel, seeing gorilla’s first attempt and any accept/reject pattern, will refine their own guess about what others might value cheaply. Monkey then responds as third proposer, now having observed two full proposals and any acceptances, and will likely try to accumulate more of the very high-value blue chips in exchange for colors they personally value less, while still leaving counterparts non-negative surplus according to their own views.

By round 1, players have seen that monkey was willing to trade red for blue at 1:1 and 2:1 terms, revealing that monkey values blue significantly and is relatively ready to part with red. Gorilla accepted a deal where they gained 2 RED for 1 BLUE, suggesting gorilla does not strongly protect blue and is comfortable holding more red at modest implied price ratios. Camel successfully bought blue from monkey 1:1 for red, learning that at least one counterpart is happy with that trade. This partial information encourages more targeted proposals: monkey is now an obvious blue-buyer and red-seller, while gorilla seems more indifferent between red and blue. Camel and monkey may each try to extract slightly better terms while still allowing the other side a non-negative gain. Gorilla, still lacking direct evidence about exact valuations, will continue to experiment with moderate trades that create visible gains on their calculator, likely leveraging the fact that others appear more attached to blue than to red.

Round 2 is the final opportunity for trade, so players may seek to lock in any last positive-surplus exchanges. Monkey’s behavior has clearly signaled a strong desire for blue and a willingness to part with red; camel’s earlier success buying blue with red hints that they also value blue relative to red; and gorilla’s acceptance of trades that move blue out in exchange for other colors shows comparatively low blue valuation. With this pattern, the remaining mutually beneficial space largely revolves around gorilla acting as a source of blue (or as a sink for red) while camel and monkey compete to accumulate blue. However, the random-recipient rule when multiple players accept still constrains very targeted deals. Proposers will typically design offers that at least one of the others finds non-negative, but overly aggressive attempts to exploit inferred preferences risk universal rejection. In these last three turns, each player weighs the small but safe gains from modest color substitutions against the chance that any risky, one-sided proposal simply gets declined and leaves their allocation unchanged at the end of the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values for red and blue chips, with green chips fixed at $0.50 for everyone. Each player starts with 10 of each color and knows only their own red/blue values, but they see all proposals and who accepts, gradually learning about others’ incentives. Alligator values red most ($0.70) and blue least ($0.30); Otter values blue most ($0.50) and red least ($0.30); Tiger sits in between with high red ($0.60) and low blue ($0.20). This creates natural trade directions: Otter wants to give up red to get blue; Alligator wants to give up blue to get red; Tiger mildly prefers red over blue but has less reason to make large trades unless terms are favorable. Green chips, being common-value at $0.50, act as a neutral or balancing currency and are unlikely to be traded unless someone mistakenly overpays or uses green to signal or sweeten a cross-color deal.

Because accept/decline decisions are made with a surplus calculator showing exact payoffs in money terms for the responding player, dominated trades (negative for a responder) will almost always be declined. Proposers must therefore structure offers that give at least a small non-negative gain to at least one potential recipient while being personally profitable. However, uncertainty about others’ private values makes it hard to know exactly how good a deal must be to induce acceptance. Early in the game, proposals are likely to be somewhat exploratory: modest-sized trades with terms that are clearly good for the proposer but potentially acceptable for others, intended to test how much counterparties value each chip color.

Strategically, proposers face a tension between generosity and competition. If an offer is too stingy, everyone declines and no surplus is realized; if it is too generous, multiple players may accept and a random recipient captures a good deal at the proposer’s expense. Proposers cannot choose which acceptor they trade with, so they must anticipate that the most advantaged counterpart (given private values) is also the most likely to accept. This encourages moderately generous but not extreme offers that are profitable across a range of plausible valuations. Over the three rounds (nine total turns), we should see: (1) early, somewhat miscalibrated proposals that test the water; (2) mid-game offers better tailored to observed behavior (who tends to accept which direction of trade); and (3) late-game attempts to lock in final gains, potentially using green sparingly to close small value gaps. Alligator will primarily seek red, Otter blue, and Tiger opportunistic improvements that exploit any mispricing, especially swapping away blue for red on favorable terms or taking small free gains in green.

The forecast below reconstructs a plausible complete playthrough consistent with the rules and with rational, value-aware responses via the surplus calculators. Proposals are chosen so that accepted trades are weakly or strictly beneficial for recipients given their true values, while declined trades would have been negative or too small to be attractive. Players never see each other’s exact valuations but infer them noisily from what gets accepted or declined, so successive turns show modest adjustments rather than perfectly optimal contracts.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has any information about others’ exact red/blue valuations, only the common range. Proposers are likely to make simple one-color-for-one-color offers that are clearly good for themselves and plausibly attractive to someone else. Because everyone starts symmetric in chip counts, trades will mostly aim to swap low-value chips for high-value ones according to the proposer’s own preferences. Alligator will tentatively try to trade away blue for red, Otter will try to trade away red for blue, and Tiger will experiment, probably also leaning toward acquiring more red in exchange for blue. Many early offers risk being declined if they leave responders worse off once they check the surplus calculator; accepted trades will indicate who values which colors more.

By round 1, some information has been revealed: Alligator accepted giving up red for blue at Otter’s terms, suggesting Alligator’s gain from red is not extremely higher than from blue, or at least that the offered surplus was strong. Otter learns that someone values blue enough to take that trade; Alligator infers Otter is happy to dump red for blue. Tiger has only seen others’ behavior and still has no direct experience trading, so remains cautious. Proposers now slightly refine their offers: Alligator will still pursue red but might be wary of overpaying in blue; Otter will keep moving red for blue but may try marginally tougher terms; Tiger will aim for safely profitable swaps, especially moving blue for red if someone will take it, or experimenting with small green offers.

Going into the final round, patterns are clearer: Otter has repeatedly succeeded in trading away red for blue with Alligator, and Tiger has not traded at all. Alligator’s repeated acceptance signals a strong desire for blue under Otter’s favorable rates, even though privately Alligator values red more; Otter likely believes both that Alligator is a reliable partner for red-to-blue swaps and that Tiger is relatively reluctant. Tiger has seen offers that were not attractive in value terms and may now suspect that others systematically misprice blue and red relative to Tiger’s own valuations. In the last three turns, each player tries to capture remaining surplus: Alligator may test a smaller, safer trade or decline to move further if terms look weak; Otter may push for one more red-for-blue exchange; and Tiger either continues to abstain or accepts only if a nearly risk-free gain arises, possibly involving green as a sweetener. Because chip totals and time are finite, players now favor unambiguous, small but positive gains over speculative or exploratory offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Everyone knows that green is always worth $0.50 per chip to all players, so green can be used as a transparent, low-friction medium of exchange. In contrast, red and blue values differ substantially: dolphin loves blue and dislikes red; frog loves red and dislikes blue; pig is moderate on both red and blue. Because players only see their own valuations, they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and accept/decline behavior.

High potential surplus exists in reassigning red and blue chips: dolphin gains a lot from acquiring blue and shedding red, frog gains from acquiring red and shedding blue, and pig can profit as an intermediary, especially because pig values blue more than frog and values red more than dolphin. Bargaining dynamics will likely involve small, relatively safe trades early as players test counterparties and learn who values which colors. Offers will tend to be one-sidedly beneficial from the sender’s perspective, but must leave at least a non-negative gain for the randomly determined recipient to be acceptable.

Uncertainty comes from two main sources: (1) incomplete information about others’ values and (2) the random tie-breaking rule when multiple players accept the same offer. Proposers cannot choose which of the accepting players will trade, so they must design offers that remain profitable regardless of which accepting player is chosen. This discourages offers that are only good when matched to one specific counterparty. Over three rounds, each with three proposal turns, players are under mild time pressure to realize gains before the game ends, making them progressively more willing to propose and accept moderately favorable trades, particularly those that improve their holdings of their high-value colors even if the marginal gains are small. The forecast below follows these incentives: early moves are exploratory and information-revealing, later moves exploit emerging knowledge of who values which colors most.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) but asymmetric private values. No one yet knows others’ valuations, so this round is dominated by exploratory proposals. Each player is likely to test simple, low-risk trades that are clearly profitable for them and plausibly attractive to at least one partner. As responses come in, they will start to infer others’ preferences: who is eager to give up which colors, and who is reluctant. Given the high complementarity between dolphin’s taste for blue and frog’s taste for red, early accepted trades will likely move some red toward frog and some blue toward dolphin. Pig, with intermediate values, has an incentive to accept deals that let them cheaply acquire chips that are likely prized by another player in later rounds, especially red and blue that can be resold for green or for the other color.

By round 1, the players have observed some revealing behavior. Dolphin and frog have already traded in a way that hints dolphin likes blue more than red, and frog is willing to give blue away to get red. Pig’s acceptance of frog’s offer gives frog some sense that pig is at least somewhat comfortable giving red for blue. With this partial information, proposals become more targeted: dolphin will keep trying to offload red in exchange for blue or possibly green; frog will try to further accumulate red while shedding blue or, secondarily, green; pig will look for arbitrage opportunities, taking blue cheaply from frog or red cheaply from dolphin when possible. However, uncertainty remains about exact valuations and about who will accept which marginal rates, so offers will still be somewhat conservative and exploratory rather than pushing extreme conversion ratios.

The final round creates a last-chance environment: no future opportunities remain, so players focus on cleaning up any remaining misallocations of red and blue. By now, observed history has made the broad preference pattern clear: dolphin is a strong blue seeker and weak red holder, frog is a strong red seeker and relatively indifferent or even negative on extra blue, and pig is flexible, able to profit from modest spreads in rates. Because of the random tie-breaking rule, each proposer now designs offers that would be acceptable and profitable regardless of whether frog or pig (or dolphin, when they are the responder) ends up as the counterparty. Small, safe trades that clearly improve both sides’ private payoffs are most likely; aggressive attempts to exploit inferred valuations will be tempered by fear that the "wrong" partner might be randomly assigned. The resulting trades in this forecast incrementally move chips closer to their highest-valued holders and exhaust the most obvious gains from trade before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips and common-valued green chips, with simultaneous accept/decline responses and random partner selection when multiple players accept. Because each player sees their own surplus calculator, no one should ever accept a trade with negative private value, and proposers will mainly search for mutually beneficial red–blue swaps or low-value-for-high-value exchanges that exploit differences in private valuations without knowing others’ exact values. Iguana values blue highly and red weakly, Owl values red highly and blue weakly, and Squid is roughly in the middle; all value green at $0.50. This creates a strong potential for surplus-generating trades where Iguana gives up red to gain blue, Owl gives up blue to gain red, and Squid opportunistically accepts deals that move them toward more blue or green and away from red when profitable. However, strategic uncertainty remains: no one knows others’ exact valuations, so proposers must infer them from observed acceptances or refusals over just three short rounds. Because recipient selection is random when multiple players accept, proposals that are positive for more than one responder may be attractive but introduce uncertainty for each responder about whether they will actually realize the opportunity. Over three rounds (nine proposal turns total), we expect early cautious, simple trades that test willingness (e.g., red-for-blue at seemingly favorable terms) and then, once at least one surplus-enhancing pattern is discovered, attempts to repeat or slightly improve on those terms. Given the values, Iguana will aim to dump red for blue or green, Owl will search for red even at the cost of blue or sometimes green, and Squid will accept moderate gains in blue or green for red while mostly rejecting trades that sacrifice blue or green for red unless the terms are very generous. The main frictions are incomplete information, limited time, and the requirement to choose a single color to buy and a single different color to sell on each turn, which may prevent perfectly efficient reallocation but should still yield several mutually beneficial swaps.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no direct information about each other’s valuations. Iguana, as the first mover, will likely test the market by offering their low-value red for either blue or green on favorable terms to entice at least one acceptance and infer others’ preferences. Owl, moving second, will try to acquire red while offloading blue, but will avoid excessively generous offers that might give away their strong red preference. Squid, moving third, will have seen two proposal/response patterns and can respond opportunistically, using early information to shape a balanced portfolio favoring blue and green. Overall, round 0 serves as exploratory bargaining, with cautious but clearly surplus-positive trades more likely to be accepted and more aggressive attempts being refused.

By round 1, players have observed that Owl readily accepted a trade giving up blue for red at a modest implied rate, revealing a strong preference for red over blue, and that Iguana was willing to trade red for blue and later to accept red in exchange for blue on terms that were at least not too unfavorable. Squid has shown a willingness to part with red to gain blue. This emerging pattern suggests to all players that red is relatively more valuable to Owl, blue is especially valuable to Iguana, and red is relatively weak for Squid. Proposers now try to refine their offers: Iguana may still attempt to trade away red for blue or green but could probe slightly better terms, Owl may push harder to get red while offering less blue, and Squid will aim to shed more red for blue while being wary of losing green or too much blue. Some offers will now be more strategic, nudging terms closer to each proposer’s ideal while still remaining weakly acceptable to at least one responder.

Entering the final round, everyone has stronger beliefs: Owl’s repeated acceptance of blue-for-red trades confirms high red value and low blue value; Iguana’s willingness both to trade away and accept red on different terms signals that red is moderately weak but not worthless; Squid’s consistent selling of red for blue clarifies that red is their weakest chip as well. With only three turns left, players are more willing to accept any small positive-surplus deals because there is no future strategic cost. Iguana will try to make a last improvement by shedding remaining red for blue or green, Owl will grab any remaining profitable red-increasing opportunities, and Squid will chase final chances to convert red into blue. Because portfolio constraints now matter (some players may be low on a color they’d like to trade), feasible surplus-improving trades become more limited and marginal, but the probability of acceptance for positively valued offers rises as the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this three-player bargaining game, all players value green chips equally at $0.50, but value red and blue chips differently. Gorilla has low value for blue ($0.10) and moderate value for red ($0.40); Llama and Whale both have high value for blue ($0.90) and somewhat higher value for red ($0.60). Everyone starts with an equal bundle of 10 of each color. Efficient trades focus on reallocating chips from low-value holders to high-value holders: especially blue chips from Gorilla to either Llama or Whale, and potentially some red chips from Gorilla to the others as well. Green chips are used as a neutral currency since they are common-value, and red/blue chips can also trade directly against each other when valuations differ sufficiently. However, nobody knows others’ exact values; they only see their own calculator. Early in the game, players are uncertain whether others value red more or less than blue or whether someone is the “low-value” holder for a given color. Thus initial proposals likely probe for willing counterparties, using small, conservative trades that show positive calculator gains for the proposer but may or may not be attractive to others. Over time, participants may infer that someone is under- or over-valuing a color if they repeatedly accept or reject certain patterns. Because responses are simultaneous and random selection breaks ties among multiple acceptors, proposers must design offers that leave enough surplus to make it attractive for at least one non-sender, but not so generous that they risk giving away value. Llama and Whale, who share identical values and both strongly want blue, are in implicit competition to capture Gorilla’s blue chips; this competition might lead Gorilla to extract better prices, but Gorilla does not directly observe their valuations and only learns through what is accepted or rejected. With just three rounds (nine total proposals) and no carryover beyond the game, players balance experimentation with securing sure gains: modest, clearly positive-sum trades on blue and red for green or for each other are especially likely, while more extreme or one-sided offers tend to be rejected. Overall, we expect a few small but value-creating trades, particularly involving Gorilla selling blue (and perhaps some red) to Llama or Whale, with some proposed trades failing as players grope toward the underlying value structure under private information and strategic uncertainty.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold symmetric endowments and have no direct information about others’ private valuations. Gorilla, seeing that blue is their weakest chip and red is moderately valuable, is naturally inclined to explore trades that shed blue or, secondarily, convert red into green if they believe others might undervalue green. Llama and Whale, each seeing high value for both red and blue, initially do not know whether the market is skewed toward or against them. Early offers are likely exploratory: Gorilla tests whether someone is willing to give up green or blue, while Llama and Whale test what mix of colors can attract a partner without giving away too much. Because this is the very first round, much of the interaction is about information gathering through acceptances and rejections, and trades may be small in size to limit downside risk while still being informative.

After round 0, Gorilla has learned that at least one partner values blue more than green, because their offer of 3 BLUE for 2 GREEN was accepted, while symmetric 2 BLUE for 2 GREEN offers by Llama and Whale were not accepted by Gorilla. Llama now holds slightly fewer green and more blue, and Gorilla holds more green and fewer blue. This pattern suggests to Llama that Gorilla is likely the low-value blue holder and is happy to accumulate green. Whale infers similarly from Gorilla’s refusal to swap green for blue. In this second round, proposers may be bolder: Gorilla can try to sell more blue at slightly better terms; Llama and Whale, realizing Gorilla may be a cheap source of blue, might push for blue-for-green trades that are slightly more favorable to them than the initial one Gorilla offered, while still leaving enough surplus that Gorilla might accept. However, they still cannot tailor offers to a specific counterpart due to random recipient selection if both accept, which keeps offers somewhat moderate.

By the start of round 2, Gorilla has twice successfully traded away blue for green on favorable terms from their perspective, reinforcing the belief for everyone that Gorilla values blue least and is happy to be paid in green. Llama and Whale each now know that blue is in high demand among themselves and that Gorilla is the primary source. However, Gorilla is also running down their blue stock; marginal gains from further blue sales shrink as Gorilla’s portfolio becomes more green-heavy and blue-scarce. With only one round left, each proposer must decide whether to attempt one more surplus-creating trade or to avoid thin-margin deals that might risk losses. Gorilla is likely to offer a smaller, still positive blue-for-green trade; Llama and Whale may shift toward testing red-for-green or red-for-blue swaps if they suspect some asymmetry in red values, but the symmetric acceptance patterns so far give them little firm evidence. Given the short horizon and modest remaining potential gains, some reasonable offers may still be rejected as players avoid ambiguous or low-surplus moves at the end of the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip counts (10 of each color) but very different private values for red and blue. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Duck values red and green equally at $0.50 and blue at $0.20; lion values red at $0.40, blue at $0.30, green at $0.50; tiger values red at $0.80, blue at $1.00, green at $0.50. From an omniscient perspective, tiger has the strongest preference for red and especially blue; duck has weak demand for blue and medium demand for red; lion’s valuations sit in between. Efficient trades tend to move blue and red chips toward tiger and away from duck and lion when those counterparties receive enough compensation in green or in their own high-value colors. However, during play each person only sees their own values, so they must infer others’ preferences from what is offered and accepted. Early in the game, proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat conservative: proposers will try to generate clear personal gains (non-negative surplus given their calculator) while leaving enough on the table that at least one counterpart may plausibly accept. Because multiple acceptances lead to a random partner selection, proposers who see both opponents as potential counterparties will design offers that yield them gains no matter which opponent accepts. Responders will only accept trades that yield them a non-negative surplus in their own calculator, and some players may require a strictly positive gain unless they see strategic value in rearranging their portfolio for future trades. Over the three rounds, players may become more aggressive as they observe revealed preferences (who accepts which colors at what rates) and as the remaining opportunity to trade shrinks, generating a mix of successful mutually beneficial deals and cautious, declined offers that appear too one-sided to the responders.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has 10 of each chip, and no private information about others’ valuations has been revealed yet. Proposers are likely to experiment with simple, roughly one-for-one or slightly favorable exchanges that give them a clear immediate gain according to their surplus calculator, while still looking plausible from at least one responder’s perspective. Since no one knows who values which color more, trades involving green (the common-value chip) are attractive as a relatively safe medium of exchange: proposers can use green to compensate others for offloading colors they personally dislike. Many early offers will be declined if they appear too skewed or if responders are unsure about counterpart preferences, but even declined offers convey information about what the sender is trying to acquire.

By the start of round 1, some information has been revealed: duck signaled at least mild interest in acquiring blue by buying it for green, lion showed willingness to exchange blue and red for green at certain rates, and tiger showed a strong willingness to give up blue for green at a generous price—hinting that tiger values green reasonably but has a high implied cost for blue. With these early signals, players can update their beliefs: lion now suspects that duck values blue less than green (due to duck’s small overpayment), and that tiger values blue highly (since tiger was willing to pay multiple greens for one blue). Players begin designing trades that exploit these perceived differences more explicitly, but they still have to keep proposals acceptable for at least one responder while guarding against the risk that the “wrong” partner accepts when both might find the terms attractive.

By round 2, the final chance to trade is approaching, so players become more willing to accept modest but safe gains rather than hold out for ideal deals. Duck now holds a portfolio slightly tilted toward red and away from blue; lion has somewhat more green and fewer blue chips; tiger is increasingly specialized in high-value blue and red while giving up some green. Everyone has a better qualitative sense that tiger strongly prefers blue and red, and that duck and lion are more neutral, but exact valuations remain private. The bargaining dynamics shift toward closing remaining arbitrage opportunities: proposers try to squeeze out final small gains by offering near break-even deals that might still be attractive to counterparts because of their different valuations or portfolio imbalances. Some proposals are still declined if they appear too unfavorable, but accepted trades now tend to be tighter (smaller surplus per party) and focused on fine-tuning each player’s holdings before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 of each chip color and privately know only their own red/blue valuations; green is common-value at $0.50 each. Bear and Goose both value red at $1.00 and blue at $0.30/$0.20 respectively; Ram values blue at $1.00 and red at $0.80. This creates clear bilateral gains from trade: Bear and Goose want to accumulate red and unload blue; Ram wants to accumulate blue and is the relatively least eager holder of red. Because green is fixed at $0.50 to everyone, it often serves as a neutral medium or side-payment. However, players do not know the others’ exact payoffs; they only observe accept/decline decisions and chip flows, and see their own surplus during each potential trade. Strategic uncertainty arises from: (1) incomplete information about others’ valuations; (2) competition between Bear and Goose to trade with Ram when buying blue; and (3) the random selection rule when multiple players accept a proposal. Early in the game, rational proposals are likely to be small, favorable-but-not-outrageous trades that test counterparties’ willingness to swap red and blue. As accept/decline patterns accumulate, players infer who is bullish on which color and adjust their offers. Bear and Goose will generally avoid giving up red unless they get a strong surplus in blue and/or green, and will be cautious offering green because it is safe value. Ram will try to sell red only at modest discounts and will be happy to offload blue at high prices in green or red. Over three rounds (nine total proposals), we should see a few executed trades that move red from Ram to Bear/Goose and blue from Bear/Goose to Ram, plus possibly minor green transfers as sweeteners, while some more aggressive offers will be declined as players protect their private value and try to learn about rivals’ preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone is symmetric in chip holdings but asymmetric in values. No one yet knows who likes which color beyond the public range info. First proposals are likely to be exploratory, with modestly favorable terms for the sender. Bear and Goose each know they love red and moderately like blue, while Ram knows they love blue and are lukewarm on red. Because no valuation information has been revealed yet, each proposer will test the waters with trades that generate a clear personal gain without being so extreme that no one could accept. Accepted or declined offers this round begin to reveal which counterparties are willing to move red or blue and at what rough exchange rates.

After round 0, some information is revealed. Ram has willingly given up blue for green and for blue–red swaps, signaling that blue may be relatively cheap for them and that they value green at least as much as blue. Goose bought red with blue, showing a strong desire for red and a willingness to shed blue.
