Bear purchased blue using green, implying Bear sees blue as at least as good as green or expects to profit by reselling blue later. Now both Bear and Goose have seen that Ram is an active trading partner for blue, and Ram has seen that Goose is willing to buy red and possibly that Bear is comfortable holding more blue. Proposers in this round can refine their offers: Bear may try to arbitrage blue into red, Goose may attempt further red accumulation, and Ram may look for better-priced opportunities to increase green or rearrange red/blue holdings.

By the final round, players have seen several acceptances and rejections: Ram has shown willingness to swap blue for green and sell red for blue; Goose has paid blue for red; Bear has both bought blue for green and then swapped blue for red with Goose. These patterns strongly suggest to everyone that red is especially valuable to Bear and Goose, while blue is especially valuable to Ram, and that green is a safe but not dominant asset. With only three proposals left, players face a last chance to improve portfolios. Proposers will be somewhat more aggressive but still constrained by private valuations: they will look for remaining win–win opportunities (e.g., Bear/Goose buying red from Ram, Ram buying blue from Bear/Goose), but will avoid trades that clearly destroy their own surplus. Some offers will be rejected as too one-sided given what has been learned, while at least one more modestly beneficial trade is likely to clear as players exploit residual misalignments in chip values.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features partially aligned but asymmetric values over red and blue chips, with green chips fixed at $0.50 for everyone. Octopus values both red and blue at $1.00 and is the only player for whom red is highly valuable; Lion and Ram both value blue at $0.70, but Lion values red at $0.50 while Ram values red only at $0.20. This creates a strong efficiency motive to move red chips from Ram (low value) toward Octopus (high value), and a secondary motive to move some red from Lion to Octopus if Octopus can compensate Lion with blue or green. Meanwhile, blue is somewhat more valuable to Octopus (1.00) than to Lion/Ram (0.70), so in principle blue should also flow toward Octopus, but Overpaying for blue risks giving too much surplus away. Green is neutral and can serve as a preferred medium of exchange since all players agree on its value. Strategic uncertainty is driven by private valuations: each player sees their own numbers but must infer others’ values from observed offers and acceptances. Proposers see their own-net surplus calculator, which encourages them not to make personally negative trades, but they also must design offers that are at least weakly attractive for at least one other player given unknown valuations. Because recipients respond simultaneously and one is randomly chosen if multiple accept, proposers cannot fully target a specific partner; instead they must craft offers likely to be appealing to any high-value holder of the requested chip type. Over three short rounds with fixed proposer order, players are under time pressure to experiment early (probing others’ valuations) and then refine toward more balanced, mutually beneficial trades, especially trades moving red toward Octopus. Nonetheless, my reconstruction assumes relatively simple, myopic behavior: each player mainly tries to exploit obviously favorable opportunities revealed by prior trades while avoiding personal losses, rather than solving for a fully efficient final allocation.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color and know only their own valuations. Everyone is still in the dark about how others rank red vs. blue, so early proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat self-serving. Lion moves first and will probably test interest in trading away a chip they value less for one they value more, but without knowing which counterpart is the best partner. Octopus, knowing they value both red and blue at $1.00, will be eager to acquire these chips from any player who seems to value them less, likely using green as flexible payment. Ram, knowing they value red very poorly, will be inclined to sell red if they can get either blue or green cheaply, but initially they also lack information about whether others value red strongly. Overall, round 0 will feature simple, probe-like trades as players begin to infer others’ relative valuations from who accepts what.

By the start of round 1, the history reveals two key facts: Ram willingly traded red for blue from Lion (implying Ram values blue more than red), and Ram also sold red to Octopus for only green (suggesting Ram views red as especially weak relative to green). Octopus’ eagerness to buy red cheaply with green signals that Octopus likely values red highly. This pattern indicates to Lion that Ram is a low-valuation red holder and that Octopus might be a high-valuation buyer of red. Ram, seeing that Octopus quickly accepted their red-for-green trade, now suspects Octopus values red strongly. These inferences push Lion and Ram to think harder about channeling surplus via trades that move red toward Octopus, with green often used as payment. Players will now propose more targeted, surplus-extracting trades: Lion tries to sell more red to Octopus or Ram for blue or green, Octopus tries to continue accumulating cheap red, and Ram tries to improve their mix of chips without overpaying for red they now believe others value more.

Going into the final round, trade history strongly suggests to everyone that Octopus values red highly and is willing to pay with green (and possibly blue), while Ram treats red as a low-value chip and has repeatedly sold it. Lion has already sold some red to Octopus for green and sees that Octopus did not accept blue-for-green proposals from others at unfavorable terms. With only three proposals left, players will mostly try to capture remaining gains from trade in red, while avoiding personally negative moves. Lion can still profitably shift a bit more red to Octopus for green at modest rates. Octopus may attempt one more acquisition of cheap red, but must be careful not to give away too much blue or green. Ram, having already offloaded much of their red, will be more cautious and only accept clearly favorable deals that improve their mix toward blue or green. In this reconstruction, the remaining trades are modest and fine-tuning rather than drastic reallocations, with players respecting their calculators and avoiding outright losses.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 for everyone) and privately valued red/blue chips. Cat and Duck both value red and blue at $1.00 and $0.70–$1.00 respectively, while Buffalo values red at $0.60 and blue at only $0.10. All start with symmetric portfolios (10 of each color) but very asymmetric preferences. Green is neutral for everyone, so it becomes the natural medium of exchange. The main efficiency gains come from moving blue chips away from Buffalo (who dislikes them) toward Cat (who loves them) and moving red chips from Buffalo toward Cat and Duck. Cat is the strongest demander for both red and blue; Buffalo is the weakest; Duck is intermediate for blue but equal to Cat on red. However, players do not know each other’s values; they only see their own, the trade history, and who accepts what. The UI makes it easy to see own-side surplus from any proposed trade, so no one will knowingly accept a trade that gives them negative value. Early in Round 0, players experiment with modest, slightly favorable offers that could plausibly be accepted by at least one opponent with different values. As the game progresses and they infer that others must like red/blue more than they do (from rejections and acceptances), proposers adjust their terms toward more balanced or even slightly generous trades to guarantee execution before the 3-round horizon ends. Uncertainty is about who values which color how much, not about one’s own payoffs. Because multiple acceptances trigger a random partner lottery, proposers avoid obviously too-generous deals that might invite competition; most trades are structured to yield a clear net gain to the proposer and a small but positive gain to at least one plausible recipient. Over three rounds, we observe Buffalo trying to dump low-value blue for green or red, Cat aggressively acquiring blue and red with green, and Duck opportunistically taking blue when cheap and giving up green or a little red when the surplus is clear.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no direct evidence about others’ valuations. Each knows only that red and blue are somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00 for everyone, and that green is fixed at $0.50. Buffalo sees that blue is nearly worthless to them ($0.10), red is slightly better ($0.60), and green is $0.50, so they want to trade away blue if possible. Cat and Duck both see that red is quite valuable ($1.00), with Cat also strongly valuing blue ($1.00) and Duck moderately valuing blue ($0.70). With this uncertainty, the natural first moves are exploratory, with moderate price ratios that would be profitable for the proposer but not so extreme that they must be rejected. Early acceptances or rejections will give coarse signals: repeated refusals to give up red for green, for example, suggest that others also like red a lot.

After a fully declined first round, everyone updates slightly: green-for-blue at roughly 1:1 and green-for-red at 1.5:1 proved unattractive. Each player infers that others probably value red and blue more than $0.50 per chip on average, especially since no one was willing to give up red cheaply. Buffalo learns that offering to dump blue for green on terms just modestly favorable to themselves is not enough; at least one opponent likely also likes blue. Cat observes that neither Buffalo nor Duck parted with green for blue at parity, so they infer that their own blue valuation may be on the high side. Duck sees that neither Buffalo nor Cat would sell red for green at the offered 1.5 green per 2 red, suggesting that red is widely seen as more valuable than green. In Round 1, players respond by sweetening their offers a bit: Buffalo makes blue cheaper, Cat offers more value for blue, and Duck tries a more balanced red-for-blue swap that could be mutually beneficial if the counterparty’s valuations differ enough.

By the final round, several trades have occurred and players have better, though still noisy, information. Buffalo has successfully dumped some blue for green and then for red, revealing to others that Buffalo’s blue valuation is likely low. Cat has bought blue from Buffalo and sold some on to Duck, indicating that Cat values blue highly but is also willing to arbitrage when Duck is willing to pay with green. Duck has exchanged blue for red with Buffalo, signaling that Duck also values red highly relative to blue, though perhaps not as highly as Cat. Everyone now has a rough picture: red is the top asset, blue is intermediate but heterogeneous in value, and green is a safe middle. With only one round left, players become more concessive: they aim for clearly positive-sum trades with at least one counterparty, even if they must give away a bit more surplus than they would have earlier, because unused chips at the end cannot be reallocated. Terms move closer to fair price ratios from each side’s perspective, yielding final rebalancing trades that move chips closer to high-value holders.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. All players start with symmetric inventories (10 of each color), but they differ in how much they personally value red and blue, which creates gains from trade: bat values red and blue at $0.80 each, fox values blue highly ($0.90) but red poorly ($0.40), and frog values blue the most ($1.00) and red moderately ($0.50). Green is worth $0.50 to everyone. Because everyone sees the same chip holdings and trade history but knows only their own values, early proposals double as signaling devices: a player’s willingness to give up a color hints at how much they value it relative to others. However, nobody can perfectly infer others’ values, and they must guess what proposals might be acceptable. The surplus calculator means that no player will knowingly accept a proposal that reduces their own payoff, so accepted trades must create non-negative surplus for both sides given their private values. With three players and random partner selection whenever more than one accepts, any proposal that is attractive to multiple players increases strategic uncertainty: proposers cannot target a particular partner, and responders must decide whether to accept despite that randomness. Over three rounds, proposers will likely experiment with conservative, roughly value-neutral offers at first (especially involving green chips, which are safe benchmarks because values are common), then adjust toward trades that move red chips away from fox and toward bat or frog, and blue chips toward frog and fox from bat, provided both sides can gain. Competition between fox and frog for blue and possible competition between bat and frog for red are likely to shape acceptance patterns, but players’ incomplete knowledge of each other’s valuations will make them cautious, especially in early turns.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, inventories are symmetric and no one has information about others’ private values beyond the common ranges. Proposers are likely to test the market with relatively small trades, often involving green as the payment medium because its value is common knowledge. These early offers serve both to capture some surplus and to probe how willing others are to give up or demand premium colors like blue and red. Because nobody wants to reveal too much or risk a sure loss, proposals are expected to be mild, close to break-even for typical valuations. Rejections will be common until players see at least one clearly profitable offer on their calculators.

By round 1, some information has leaked through observed trades: bat’s willingness to swap green for blue suggests bat values blue at least as much as green, and fox’s acceptance implies blue is relatively valuable to fox as well. Frog’s later purchase of red from fox using green indicates frog values red more than green, and fox is relatively happy to part with red at a slight gain. However, exact valuations are still unknown, so players continue to propose trades that appear moderately favorable but not extreme. Fox now holds fewer green and more red, while frog holds more red and fewer green; bat holds slightly more blue and fewer green. These imbalances, plus the knowledge that blue and red seem desired by others, push the proposers toward using green as payment and trying to accumulate their personally higher-value colors, while remaining wary of overpaying.

Entering round 2, inventories reflect several executed trades: bat has shifted toward more high-value red and blue while giving up green, fox has converted some lower-value red into higher-value blue and spent green, and frog has increased red holdings at the cost of green and possibly passed on marginal blue offers. With only one proposal turn left for each player, this final round is a last chance to secure small, clearly profitable trades. Players now have a clearer qualitative sense that red is relatively cheap for fox and more valuable to bat and frog, and that blue is especially attractive to fox and frog. Proposers will likely make narrowly beneficial offers that fit these inferred patterns, but responders, aware the game is ending, will reject anything not obviously improving their payoff. As a result, only trades that are very nearly guaranteed to be surplus-improving for both sides are likely to be accepted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips and privately valued red and blue chips. Parrot and Turtle value blue at the top of the public range ($1.00), while Dog values both red and green at $0.50 and blue at only $0.30. Parrot values red at $0.80, slightly above Turtle’s $0.60 and well above Dog’s $0.50. Because players see only their own values, they begin uncertain about whether others share their preferences or have different ones. Parrot and Turtle each know that blue is very valuable to them; they may initially assume blue is also relatively valuable to others, making them hesitant to give up blue cheaply. Dog knows blue is their worst chip and will be inclined to sell blue for red or even green when they can get positive surplus. Since green is common-value and always worth $0.50, none of the players has a value advantage in trading green; trades involving green will only occur if someone believes others value green differently or uses green to signal willingness to trade. Strategic uncertainty arises from not knowing others’ valuations for red and blue and from the random recipient rule when multiple players accept. Because each player gets three proposal turns and cannot direct which accepter they trade with, they must design proposals that are attractive enough to at least one partner but not so generous that they give away surplus. Over time, observed acceptance or rejection of offers will give weak clues about others’ valuations (for example, fast acceptance of offers that sell blue might indicate a relatively low blue value for the proposer), but with only three rounds there is limited scope for learning. In this forecasted playthrough, Dog gradually discovers that their cheap blue chips are attractive to the others, while Parrot and Turtle experiment with buying blue and occasionally red from each other, constrained by the risk that a favorable deal might be taken by the ‘wrong’ partner due to randomization among multiple accepters.

rounds.round_explanation: Everyone starts with symmetric chip quantities (10 of each color) but asymmetric values. In this first round, players are basically guessing about others’ valuations. Dog knows blue is their least valuable chip and tries early to trade away blue for red or green, but will be conservative because they do not know that the others value blue at the maximum. Parrot and Turtle each know blue is very valuable to themselves and slightly prefer red to green, but they do not know whether Dog shares those valuations. They may initially test modest exchanges that do not risk large losses even if their counterpart values are lower than expected.

After round 0, no trades have yet occurred with Dog, but Parrot has successfully bought blue from Turtle in a slightly unfavorable deal for Turtle. This suggests (to Turtle) that Parrot values blue a lot, and to Parrot that Turtle is at least somewhat willing to sell blue. Dog sees unwillingness by others to give up red cheaply for blue, hinting that they may also value blue highly. Now players adjust: Dog will still try to monetize their low-value blue, but may offer slightly better terms. Parrot, having gained blue at a good price, can try to buy more blue if available or occasionally buy red if it seems underpriced. Turtle may become more cautious about selling blue after realizing they made a small loss in round 0.

Going into the final round, Parrot has successfully bought four blue chips total (two from Turtle, two from Dog), revealing to Dog and Turtle that Parrot is a strong blue buyer. Dog has profited from selling some blue for red and is now more confident that blue is relatively more valuable to others than to themselves. Turtle failed to complete any further trades after the first, which may make them cautious but also aware that both Parrot and Dog are willing to move red and blue at certain ratios. With only one proposal turn left for each, players must decide whether to push for one more profitable but plausible deal rather than experiment. Risk of rejection is less costly now because there is no future to preserve.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values over red and blue chips, with common-value green chips. Each player starts with 10 chips of each color and knows only their own chip values, but they can infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptance behavior. Badger values BLUE (0.90) > RED (0.60) > GREEN (0.50), Peacock values RED (1.00) >> GREEN (0.50) >> BLUE (0.10), and Unicorn values BLUE (1.00) >> GREEN (0.50) >> RED (0.40). Socially efficient trades convert chips away from low-value owners toward high-value owners: RED should flow toward Peacock (who values it most) and away from Unicorn; BLUE should flow toward Unicorn (and Badger) and away from Peacock; GREEN is neutral at 0.50 for everyone and essentially a low-risk medium of exchange. However, because each player only sees their own values, they must experiment and infer who likes what, while also worrying about random partner selection when multiple players accept their offer. This randomness weakens targeted bargaining power: you can’t guarantee that the partner who best fits your interests will be the one who trades with you. Over three rounds, everyone gets three proposal turns, and each proposal is a simple one-commodity-for-another offer. The shared surplus calculator ensures that no one will knowingly accept a negative-surplus trade; accepted trades must provide nonnegative gains to both sides. Early in the game, players are likely to make exploratory offers with greens as a neutral currency and relatively small quantities to test counterparties’ acceptance thresholds. As they observe who eagerly trades which colors, they gradually form beliefs: Peacock probably reveals a strong appetite for red, Unicorn for blue, and Badger for blue as well. Because there are only three rounds, players have limited time to fully optimize; we should expect modest, mutually beneficial trades that move chips partially toward their highest-value owners, rather than fully efficient specialization.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players still have symmetric endowments (10 of each color) and almost no information about others’ values. Proposals are likely to be small, conservative tests that use green as a neutral medium and probe for which colors others are willing to part with. Badger, moving first, will likely try a mildly favorable trade that increases their high-value blue or sheds relatively low-value green, while ensuring it looks acceptable to at least one counterpart. Peacock will then experiment with acquiring red, attempting to see if anyone undervalues it. Unicorn, last, will aim to cheaply acquire blue or unload red, but must account for the fact that any acceptable partner might be chosen at random when more than one accepts, introducing uncertainty into targeting.

By round 1, some information has emerged from the first three proposals. Peacock has revealed a willingness to give up blue to gain red, hinting that Peacock values red highly and blue relatively little. Unicorn’s acceptance of that deal indicates that Unicorn is happy to trade away red for blue, consistent with high blue and low red value. Badger’s acceptance of Unicorn’s red-for-blue offer further reveals Badger’s taste for blue. Players now suspect: Peacock is a red lover and blue hater; Unicorn is a blue lover and red disliker; Badger also likes blue. Green remains a flexible, safe medium. With updated beliefs, proposals now focus more on pushing chips along these suspected value gradients: Peacock tries harder to collect red, Badger and Unicorn both try to accumulate blue, and Unicorn continues to unload red. Strategic tension arises because Badger and Unicorn both covet blue, while Peacock mainly acts as the low-value blue supply. Proposers must now design offers that are attractive enough to specific partners while remaining robust to the random tie-breaking if both potential recipients would benefit.

Entering the final round, each player has clearer beliefs: Peacock strongly values red and is willing to part with blue and some green; Unicorn highly values blue and is comfortable offloading red; Badger values blue but is also fine using green as a payment vehicle. Everyone faces endgame incentives: there is no reason to hold back for future bargaining, so any nonnegative-surplus deal may be considered. However, inventories have become somewhat skewed by prior trades, limiting what each can realistically offer without overshooting their own value constraints. The remaining opportunities mainly involve Peacock shedding low-value blue or green to gain more red from Unicorn, and Badger trying to squeeze a little more blue from either Peacock or Unicorn in exchange for green or moderate-value red. Players will propose slightly more aggressive, but still positive-sum, trades that push chips closer to their highest-value owners before the game ends, while avoiding any net-loss deals flagged clearly by the surplus calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player bargaining game with incomplete information about private chip values for red and blue but common knowledge that green is worth $0.50 to everyone. Each player starts with 10 of each chip color. Because all players know that green is common-value and relatively high compared to plausible red/blue values, green acts as a natural medium of exchange and a benchmark for value. Owl has the highest blue value (0.70) and thus is likely to be eager to acquire blue and willing to part with red or possibly green to do so, while mouse has relatively higher blue than red (0.40 vs 0.40 equal, but both lower than owl’s blue) and dog is symmetrical between red and blue at 0.50. From the analyst’s perspective, the most efficient trades move blue chips toward owl and keep green widely distributed. However, players do not know each other’s valuations, so they must infer preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. Proposers use the surplus calculator to avoid negative-value trades for themselves and often start with conservative offers that are favorable to them but may be rejected. Responders accept exactly those offers with strictly positive private surplus, unless they are strategically waiting for better offers or trying to conceal information; however, with only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, the scope for sophisticated signaling is limited. We should expect early-round proposals to be somewhat exploratory and possibly declined, followed by modest adjustments as players learn who seems interested in which colors. Randomization between multiple simultaneous acceptances adds uncertainty: a proposer cannot reliably target a specific trading partner, which pushes them to propose deals that would be acceptable to at least one partner but perhaps not too generous. Over three rounds, we forecast a modest number of accepted trades, primarily involving owl acquiring blue and giving up green or red, with dog and mouse occasionally exploiting their ability to sell green (which they value equally) at terms that trade off against red or blue at favorable private prices. Because all players know their own green value is relatively high, they are cautious about giving up green, so the most plausible trades are small, incremental exchanges that generate clear, visible surplus for at least one responder.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has revealed any information about their private red/blue values. Proposers are likely to test the waters with simple, small trades that look privately profitable and that they hope might appeal to at least one counterpart. Because everyone knows green is common-value and relatively safe, early trades may involve offering a bit of green to pull in red or blue, or vice versa, at ratios that reflect each proposer’s own private valuations. We should see conservative offers which may be slightly too demanding, leading to some early declines as responders calibrate what kinds of deals might be available.

By the start of round 1, owl has successfully traded 3 green to mouse for 2 blue, revealing owl’s strong demand for blue and willingness to give up green at a generous rate. Mouse’s acceptance suggests that mouse is not particularly attached to green relative to blue, or at least finds the offered ratio slightly favorable. Dog, who did not participate in the first accepted trade, now observes that owl values blue and may infer that blue is relatively more valuable to owl than to typical players. This round is likely to feature more targeted attempts to trade blue with owl, possibly at slightly improved terms for the counterparts, and some experimentation with red as an additional dimension for surplus creation. Players remain cautious but should start to converge toward mutually acceptable ratios.

Going into the final round, owl has clearly signaled a strong appetite for blue, having already traded away green and red to obtain blue, while dog and mouse have mostly held on to their green and traded cautiously. With only three turns left, players may become slightly more willing to propose and accept thin-margin trades rather than risk ending with unexploited surplus. Dog may try one more time to exploit owl’s blue demand or trade red/blue at near-indifference rates, mouse might look for a last small gain after its earlier successful trade, and owl may either push for one more blue-acquisition attempt or accept a trade that slightly reduces blue holdings in exchange for replenishing green or red if the terms are attractive enough. However, proposals that impose a clear loss on responders will still be rejected, so accepted trades are likely to be ones that are modestly beneficial for at least one responder and not too generous from the proposer’s perspective.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with symmetric chip endowments (10 of each color) but slightly different private values. Alligator and Buffalo have identical valuations: RED=$1.00, BLUE=$0.70, GREEN=$0.50; Rabbit values RED and BLUE slightly less (RED=$0.90, BLUE=$0.60, GREEN=$0.50). Everyone publicly knows greens are $0.50 to everyone and that reds/blues are worth something in [0.10,1.00], but no one knows others’ exact numbers. Because green is common-value and strictly dominated by both red and blue for all players, it is the natural medium of exchange: players are inclined to give up green to acquire red (or blue) and reluctant to give up red for green without a favorable quantity ratio. However, proposals must involve trading one color for a different color, and trading among players with very similar valuations yields little or no gain, especially when the proposer already sees on-screen whether their own surplus is positive. The key profitable direction is from Rabbit (who has the lowest values) to Alligator or Buffalo (who have higher values): Alligator/Buffalo gain more from each RED/BLUE they receive than Rabbit loses, so mutually beneficial trades exist if Rabbit can be induced to sell at a ratio that still shows Rabbit a small positive surplus. But Rabbit’s calculator only shows Rabbit’s own gain/loss, not the others’ gains, so Rabbit will accept only if the ratio is personally profitable, which compresses or eliminates available surplus for the high-valuers. The random-recipient rule when multiple players accept makes it harder for a proposer to tailor offers between the two high-valuers, so they may design offers that could work with either counterpart. Over only three rounds (nine total proposals), players have limited time to learn about others’ valuations from acceptance behavior. A likely pattern is that early proposals are exploratory and somewhat conservative (trying obviously favorable-to-others but at least non-negative-surplus deals), many are declined as too generous or too demanding, and players gravitate toward modest green-for-red or green-for-blue trades, especially involving Rabbit as the counterparty. Because Alligator and Buffalo share identical values, they tend to behave similarly, sometimes competing to accept good deals from Rabbit. The most probable outcome of a single short game is very few accepted trades, with at most one or two modest green-for-red or green-for-blue deals, primarily between Rabbit and one of the high-valuers. The forecast below reconstructs a plausible playthrough consistent with these incentives, the limited information, and the UI feedback on own-surplus for each potential trade.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has observed any behavior yet, so proposals are mostly exploratory and conservative. Alligator and Buffalo suspect that at least one other player might value red or blue less than they do, especially Rabbit, but they do not know who for sure. Rabbit knows they could be the low-valuer and tentatively tries to convert some lower-value chips into higher-value ones while keeping proposals that clearly show a personal surplus. Because everyone sees their own net gain immediately in the calculator, strongly negative-surplus moves are essentially never proposed or accepted. The dynamics are: Alligator tests whether someone will sell red cheaply for green; Buffalo tries a more targeted green-for-red trade aiming for Rabbit; Rabbit experiments with giving up blue to obtain red from a high-valuer at a ratio that still benefits Rabbit. Only offers that show clear personal gain to a responder have a chance of being accepted.

After round 0, everyone has learned something. Alligator successfully traded with Rabbit on a fairly generous green-for-red ratio, revealing that Rabbit is willing to sell red for green at 3:2. Buffalo sees that Alligator values red enough to propose that deal, and that Rabbit is not extremely protective of red. This suggests Rabbit likely has slightly lower red value than Alligator/Buffalo. Rabbit, having gained from the first trade, now holds fewer reds and more greens and is more cautious about letting go of additional red without a very favorable ratio. The strategic tension: Alligator and Buffalo want to buy more red from Rabbit but must still offer Rabbit a personal surplus; Rabbit would like to flip some green back into red or possibly into blue but is not eager to worsen their red scarcity. As a result, proposals begin to sharpen around green-for-red and blue-for-red swaps, but Rabbit now rejects ratios that are only weakly beneficial or that move Rabbit too far away from red.

By the final round, players have updated further. Two accepted trades have occurred: one where Rabbit sold red for green to Alligator, and one where Rabbit later bought red back from Buffalo using green, both at a 3 green for 2 red ratio. This symmetric pattern suggests to all players that roughly break-even deals for Rabbit involve red-for-green around 1:1.5 in quantity, which lines up with Rabbit’s lower but still relatively high red value. Alligator and Buffalo have seen Rabbit both sell and buy red at this ratio, which hints that Rabbit’s value is just under theirs but close. With only one round left, the incentive to experiment shrinks, and players mostly tweak around previously observed ratios. Alligator cautiously tries to move a bit more green into red, Buffalo attempts a blue-for-green test trade that could reveal blue valuations, and Rabbit, seeing limited time, offers one last green-for-red deal that still leaves Rabbit with a personal gain. Given slight risk aversion and uncertainty about exact thresholds, only the most clearly profitable final offer is likely to be accepted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this three-player, three-round bargaining game, each player starts symmetric in chip holdings (10 of each color) but asymmetric in how much they privately value red and blue chips. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so trade in green mostly reallocates risk and expectations, not private-value gains. Goose strongly prefers red to blue (RED $0.90 vs BLUE $0.50), Bear is indifferent across all colors (all $0.50), and Buffalo values red more than blue but with a smaller spread (RED $0.80 vs BLUE $0.20). This structure creates natural gains from trade: Goose and Buffalo are both red-loving relative to blue, while Bear is neutral and thus can serve as a middleman, being willing to trade red away if compensated in other colors. Because everyone observes only chip counts and not others’ private values, each player must infer others’ valuations from revealed behavior: which chip colors they try to buy or sell and at what implied exchange rates. Rational proposals will typically try to exchange blue chips for red chips at terms that look favorable given the proposer’s own values but that still yield a non-negative gain (or at least a non-loss) for a counterparty, as flagged by the on-screen surplus calculators. Goose, with the strongest red preference, has the highest incentive to accumulate red, ideally by offloading blue. Buffalo may similarly want more red but is more likely to exploit trades that dump low-value blue for higher-valued red in their own metric, potentially clashing with Goose in targeting the same counterparties. Bear, valuing all chips equally, has the cleanest benchmark: any strictly profitable trade in Bear’s calculator (i.e., ending up with more total valued chips or a favorable color rebalancing if Bear infers others’ overpay for red) is worth considering. However, Bear may also strategically decline offers that yield zero or negligible apparent benefit, especially if there is hope of better proposals later. Uncertainty about others’ valuations means early rounds are partly exploratory, with players testing simple, near-fair rates in common-value terms to see where others are unexpectedly eager to accept. Because proposals are limited to one color bought and one color sold per turn, and trades are executed with a single randomly chosen accepting partner, proposers must anticipate multi-accept scenarios: overly generous trades to a popular color may result in random matching, sometimes with a less-preferred partner. Over three rounds (nine total proposal turns), strategic players will gradually refine beliefs: consistent attempts to buy red or consistent eagerness to accept blue→red offers suggest a red-high type. As the game progresses and remaining chip inventories change, surplus opportunities shrink and proposals may shift from exploratory to exploitative: players who have inferred that others really value red can try to sell red at very favorable rates, or conversely, try to acquire the last cheap red before others notice. Yet, with only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, the game remains short, and many potential surplus-enhancing trades will likely remain unrealized because of coordination risk, randomization over multiple acceptances, and private-value uncertainty.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no history and thus treat the environment as symmetric in holdings but uncertain in values. Goose, knowing they value red highly, is likely to test whether others are unusually eager to buy or sell red by offering what looks like a mildly favorable rate in their own terms. Bear, being color-neutral, will experiment with simple near-even trades to see what gets accepted, treating acceptance intensity as a signal of hidden values. Buffalo, with moderate red preference and low blue value, will likely probe whether anyone is willing to give up red for Buffalo’s low-valued blue, possibly at a slight discount to entice an acceptance while keeping a clear private gain. All three want to keep early offers modest so as not to accidentally reveal too much or give away too much surplus before they understand the others’ preferences.

After round 0, only one trade executed: Buffalo traded 4 BLUE for 2 RED with Bear. Publicly, everyone now sees that Bear gave up red and took blue, while Goose did not engage. Goose can reasonably infer that Bear is at least somewhat willing to part with red, possibly suggesting Bear doesn’t prize red especially highly. Buffalo reveals a taste for red over blue through their accepted trade. Bear, from private payoffs, knows the last trade was slightly favorable or at least acceptable given Bear’s neutrality, and observes that Buffalo was willing to shed a lot of blue for red, reinforcing the belief that Buffalo values red. Goose now has a clearer target in Bear as a potential red seller, and Buffalo may try to repeat or slightly improve a similar structure, though Bear may adjust expectations and become pickier. With more information, proposals shift from purely exploratory to slightly more exploitative, but players still face uncertainty about Goose’s exact preferences and where Bear’s real reservation rates lie.

Going into the final round, Goose has successfully acquired some red from Bear; Buffalo has already executed one red-buying trade with Bear but failed to secure more. Public history shows Bear as the only consistent red seller and both Goose and Buffalo as red buyers. Bear now knows that red is in high demand and may try to improve terms, but with only one proposal left for each player, there is limited room to maneuver. Goose, having achieved some red accumulation, may push once more to acquire red from Bear at similar or slightly worse terms, banking on Bear’s acceptance if it still appears close to neutral or marginally beneficial in Bear’s calculator. Buffalo, seeing reluctance to accept their last blue-for-red offer, might either sweeten the deal or shift to a different color trade, but the short horizon and Bear’s updated beliefs reduce the odds of another favorable red-for-blue trade. Players now tend to propose more aggressive, surplus-grabbing offers, aware that there is no future retaliation or learning, while responders may become tighter, declining anything that does not show a clear positive gain in their own calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 for everyone) and privately valued red and blue chips. Peacock values red highly ($0.80) and blue moderately ($0.50); Cat values red and green evenly ($0.50) and blue low ($0.30); Otter values red ($0.70) much more than blue ($0.20). All start with 10 of each color. Because green is common-value and starts symmetric, most surplus comes from rearranging red and blue so they flow from players who value them less to those who value them more. From an analyst’s perspective, efficient trade patterns are: red should move from Cat (0.50) to Peacock (0.80) and Otter (0.70); blue should move from Peacock (0.50) to Cat (0.30) or Otter (0.20) only if compensated in higher-valued colors like red or green. However, players see only their own chip values and must infer others’ valuations from proposals and acceptances. Proposers will typically search for trades that their surplus calculator shows as profitable and that they believe could also be at least weakly attractive to someone else, but they do not know which opponent—if both accept, the recipient is random. This random-matching risk makes players reluctant to propose extremely generous deals to any specific counterpart. Over three rounds, players are likely to start cautiously, offering safe, slightly positive-surplus deals using green (known to be valuable to everyone) as a medium of exchange. As the game progresses and some valuations become partially revealed through behavior, proposals can become more targeted: for example, Cat may try to offload low-value blue while acquiring more valuable red or green, and Otter may similarly seek red in exchange for blue. Because there are only three rounds and each player has a fixed proposer schedule, there is little time for complex reputation strategies; instead, players aim for a few clearly positive trades, avoiding deals that their calculator shows as a loss. Some potentially efficient trades may not occur because of incomplete information and coordination risk—especially when two potential partners might accept but the proposer would strictly prefer one over the other yet cannot choose between them. The outcome is likely to be a modest number of accepted trades improving individual payoffs but falling short of full Pareto efficiency.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip. No one has observed any behavior yet, so beliefs about others’ valuations are very diffuse. Proposers will rely heavily on the surplus calculator for their own side but must guess how attractive a trade is to others. Green is known to be $0.50 for everyone, so it is a natural, safe currency to anchor early proposals. Peacock moves first and is likely to explore small green-for-red or green-for-blue trades, trying to gain red (which Peacock values highly) without offering too generous a surplus to unknown partners. Cat then moves, with an incentive to convert low-value blue into either red or green. Otter, going last, knows only the prior trades and may infer small signals about others’ preferences from what was offered or accepted. Because everyone is cautious and symmetry is high, we expect one or two conservative accepted trades that clearly improve both sides’ value, with most proposals clustered around green against either red or blue.

By the start of round 1, Cat has traded away some red to Peacock and some green to Otter, gaining green from Peacock and red from Otter. This pattern reveals partial information: Cat is willing to exchange red for green at 1:1 and to give green for red at 1:1, suggesting Cat views red and green as roughly similar in value. Peacock and Otter, both having paid green to acquire red, likely look red-hungry to Cat. All players have now seen that green is actively used as a medium of exchange, confirming its common value. As a result, proposers in this round can be slightly more ambitious: Cat, now moving first, may try to reshape their portfolio away from low-value blue. Peacock, going second, might attempt to offload green (which Peacock values only at $0.50) for even more high-value red, while staying wary of giving too much surplus. Otter, still valuing red strongly, may look for further chances to turn green or blue into red. However, uncertainty about blue’s value to others persists, so proposals involving blue may be more conservative or get declined if perceived as unbalanced.

Entering the final round, some broad patterns are clear. Both Peacock and Otter have repeatedly been willing to pay green to acquire red, so Cat can infer that red is relatively valuable to them. However, nobody has observed strong demand for blue; proposals that tried to trade blue for green have been declined, hinting that others likely value blue low. With only one proposal left for each player, there is limited time to experiment. Proposers now focus on clearly positive trades according to their calculators, while also considering that any accepted trade locks in their final payoff. Peacock is now quite red-heavy relative to green and may be reluctant to sell red back. Cat, sitting between high-valuing red players, could in principle profit by arbitraging red using green, but lacks precise value information and fears overpaying. Otter may try one more time to exploit any mispricing of green versus red. Because of the short horizon and uncertainty around blue, remaining trades concentrate on red–green combinations and are likely modest in size. Some efficient trades will still be left on the table because players cannot fully coordinate or observe others’ valuations in just three rounds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so GREEN trades among them do not create or destroy surplus; they only redistribute opportunities to convert RED/BLUE into value. The private values on RED and BLUE create the main gains from trade. Badger values both RED and BLUE at $1.00 (high for RED, high for BLUE). Squid values BLUE at $1.00 but RED only at $0.10 (BLUE-specialist, dislikes RED). Camel values BLUE at $0.80 and RED at $0.40 (moderate for both). Thus, efficient reallocation pushes RED chips from Squid (who undervalues them) toward Badger (who values them most), and some BLUE chips from Badger toward Squid (who values them equally highly) and Camel (who values them more than Badger values RED, but less than Squid values BLUE). However, nobody knows others’ exact values, only the public range, so proposals must be exploratory and strategically priced. Proposers see their own net gain from any tentative proposal but must guess what terms others will accept. Responders see their own net gain for the posted trade and will reject anything that yields a negative payoff. Because acceptance decisions are simultaneous and random tie-breaking picks one accepting partner, proposers must balance offering strong-enough surplus to attract at least one partner against not giving away too much. Over three rounds (nine turns), players will likely: (1) rapidly discover that Squid is eager to unload RED for BLUE/ GREEN, (2) observe that Badger is willing to pay generously for RED (since it is very valuable to Badger), and (3) see Camel often acting as an intermediary, taking modest gains where possible. By the end of the game, we expect Badger to accumulate more RED, Squid to accumulate more BLUE and some extra GREEN, and Camel to have slightly improved holdings from opportunistic trades, with a pattern of progressively more targeted proposals as players react to what was accepted or declined earlier.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has symmetric chip holdings and only beliefs from the public value ranges. Proposals are likely to be conservative and exploratory: Squid will try to trade away low-value RED for higher-value BLUE or neutral GREEN, Badger will tentatively buy RED with GREEN or BLUE, and Camel will test mild arbitrage trades. Because no one yet knows others’ exact values, they avoid extreme demands and aim for modest positive surplus that still seems plausibly acceptable.

Going into round 1, only one trade has executed: Badger bought RED from Camel using GREEN. This publicly reveals that Badger is willing to pay at least some GREEN for RED and that Camel is willing to let go of RED for GREEN at roughly that rate. Squid’s failed RED-for-BLUE offer suggests Squid might be undervaluing RED or overpricing BLUE. Proposers now slightly update beliefs: they see Badger as a strong RED buyer and Camel as flexible. Squid will likely make smaller, more balanced trades; Badger may again buy RED at favorable but not extreme terms; Camel may seek to rebalance BLUE and GREEN to exploit perceived value differences.

By round 2, the history shows a clear pattern: Badger repeatedly buys RED with GREEN and never sells RED; Squid was willing to sell RED cheaply for BLUE and also to sell RED for GREEN; Camel has twice been on the RED-selling/BLUE-buying side. This suggests to everyone that Badger overvalues RED relative to others, Squid highly values BLUE and is eager to dump RED, and Camel is in the middle. With only three proposal turns left, each player tries to lock in final gains: Squid still tries to convert remaining RED into BLUE or GREEN at safe, modest rates; Badger again seeks RED for GREEN; and Camel looks for arbitrage that exploits Squid’s and Badger’s inferred valuations. Nonetheless, not every final proposal will clear, because players now have somewhat sharper beliefs and will not accept trades too close to break-even or below.

Because the most obvious win–win trades (dog giving up red/blue for green) have already been heavily exploited, marginal trades now offer smaller individual gains, and players become pickier. This public history suggests that red may be especially valuable to at least one other player, and that duck may be a good source of red. Proposers in round 1 can exploit this by making more finely tuned red–green trades, possibly sweetening terms slightly to ensure acceptance while still making positive surplus.

By the start of round 2, duck and whale have traded twice: first 3 RED for 2 GREEN, then 2 RED for 2 GREEN. In this final round, players anticipate no future opportunities, so they are willing to accept any remaining strictly positive-surplus trades, but proposers may push terms harder knowing it is the last chance. parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) but asymmetric private values for red and blue. Green is common-value at $0.50, so its role is relatively transparent: nobody will knowingly give up green at worse than 1:1 value in their own terms, and it functions as a safe, neutral chip that can be used to lubricate trades. The main bargaining tension is over red and blue: zebra values red highly and blue moderately (RED 0.80, BLUE 0.30), gorilla values blue more than red (BLUE 0.40, RED 0.20), and turtle values blue highly and red very low (BLUE 0.70, RED 0.10). From an analyst standpoint, zebra is a natural buyer of red and seller of blue, turtle is a natural buyer of blue and seller of red, and gorilla sits in the middle but is closer to turtle on blue and closer to zebra on red. Efficient reallocation would move red from turtle (and somewhat from gorilla) to zebra, and move blue from zebra (and somewhat from gorilla) to turtle. However, players only know their own values, so they face uncertainty about how much others value each color and must infer that from observed offers. Simultaneous, private responses and random partner selection among multiple acceptors create competition effects: a sender cannot target a specific counterparty, and potential recipients must anticipate whether they might share an attractive offer with a rival. Many profitable trades (from the analyst’s viewpoint) are small, simple exchanges that clearly show gains on each side’s surplus calculator, yet imperfect value inference and risk of helping a future competitor can cause some individually efficient trades to be declined. Over three rounds, each player gets three chances to propose. Early proposals are likely to be exploratory, using conservative 1:1 or near-1:1 value ratios in the sender’s own terms to avoid visibly losing money. As chips move, later proposals can react to revealed preferences: if someone repeatedly tries to buy a color, others infer that color is valuable to them and may demand better terms. Given the short horizon and symmetric initial holdings, the game is likely to feature a small number of accepted, modestly positive-sum trades that move red toward zebra and blue toward turtle, but not achieve full efficiency.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have 10 of each chip and no direct evidence about others’ valuations. Proposals in this round are mainly exploratory and conservative, using the surplus calculator to ensure non-negative value for the sender while trying to induce at least one counterpart to accept. Because everyone is still symmetrically endowed, nobody is under immediate pressure to move, but each acceptance can reveal something about relative preferences. Zebra is likely to start by trying to improve their red holdings or shed less-valued blue. Gorilla will look for modest improvements in blue while not giving up too much green or blue value, and turtle will seek to shed low-value red and acquire high-value blue or at least neutral green.

After round 0, some information has effectively leaked: turtle’s willingness to give up red for blue at favorable terms to gorilla suggests turtle values blue a lot more than red. Gorilla’s accepted trade indicates a mild preference shift toward blue and away from red, and zebra’s and gorilla’s refusals in other offers hint that they are not desperate for green at those prices. Now holdings are asymmetric: turtle is slightly richer in blue and poorer in red, gorilla holds more red and less blue, and zebra’s portfolio is unchanged. Proposers can update expectations: zebra may infer that others particularly like blue, making it easier to sell blue for red or green; gorilla may try to arbitrage their new red holdings; turtle may try again to turn red into high-value blue or at worst neutral green, but must be mindful that the others have just seen them give away red cheaply, which might embolden tougher counteroffers.

By the final round, trade history clearly signals that turtle strongly prefers blue to red and is happy to shed red cheaply, while gorilla has twice accepted trades that convert their less-valued red into more-valued blue or green. Zebra has not traded yet but has revealed an interest in acquiring red, given two prior attempts to buy red. Portfolio asymmetries are larger now: turtle has relatively more blue and green and very little red; gorilla holds increased red from turtle offset by some loss of green and blue; zebra still has their original symmetric holdings. With only three proposals left in the game, players face a closing window to improve payoffs. Zebra may attempt a more generous but still self-profitable offer to finally secure red. Gorilla might try a small final arbitrage, trading red for blue or green if any surplus remains. Turtle, rich in blue and green and nearly out of red, has fewer strongly profitable trades available and may either passively decline poor offers or attempt a last minor reshuffle that is unlikely to move others, given the short horizon and thin remaining gains from trade.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Each player starts with an equal endowment (10 of each color), but they value red and blue differently: cat-green-4185 (cat) values blue moderately (0.70) and red/good greens equally (0.50); iguana-yellow-3262 (iguana) values red highly (0.80) and blue weakly (0.30); zebra-pink-2457 (zebra) values blue highly (0.80) and red weakly (0.40). Everyone values green at 0.50. The joint-surplus logic is straightforward: trades that convert chips from a low-value holder to a high-value holder in that color create surplus (e.g., red from cat or zebra to iguana; blue from iguana to cat or zebra). However, players only observe their own values, so they do not know which colors others value more, only that others’ red/blue values lie somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00. They must infer preferences from observed proposals and acceptances.

Key incentives: iguana is naturally a red-buyer and blue-seller; zebra is a blue-buyer and red-seller; cat is in between, willing to buy blue from someone who undervalues it and sell red to someone who overvalues it. All three are indifferent about holding vs. trading green at the margin, but because greens are common-value and symmetric, greens can act as a neutral medium of exchange or a way to hide private information about red/blue valuations.

Strategically, each proposer sees a calculator that reveals their personal net gain or loss for any contemplated trade, and each responder sees their own net impact before accepting. This tends to push behavior toward individually rational, non-negative-surplus trades. However, uncertainty about others’ valuations and the random partner selection when multiple players accept create risk: a proposer may unintentionally give a very good deal to someone who values the chips more than expected and lock in a weaker personal gain than they could have achieved with more targeted offers. Senders may also experiment in early rounds with somewhat conservative offers that they expect at least one other player to accept, then refine their strategy based on who actually takes which trades.

Given three rounds with three turns per round, and an exogenous proposal schedule (cat, iguana, zebra each round), we expect early-round experimentation with simple two-color trades (red↔blue or red/blue↔green) that seem modestly profitable from the proposer’s perspective. As players observe who eagerly accepts which trades, they update beliefs: iguana will increasingly reveal as someone who wants red and is willing to shed blue; zebra will reveal interest in acquiring blue; cat will be positioned to intermediate between them if they notice patterns. Across the full game, most accepted trades are likely to move red toward iguana and blue toward zebra (and sometimes cat), with green occasionally used as a balancing chip. Because each turn’s outcome affects later holdings, later proposals will exploit emerging imbalances—such as someone becoming red-poor or blue-rich—while still respecting private-value uncertainty. The forecast below reconstructs one plausible full game path using these incentives and observational learning, with all players avoiding trades that the calculator shows as personally loss-making.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has yet observed anyone else’s trades, so beliefs about others’ valuations are diffuse. Proposers will likely start with conservative, individually profitable offers that test the waters. Each player knows their own valuations and sees that green is a neutral 0.50 per chip, so using red/blue against green is a low-risk way to probe whether others relatively like or dislike specific colors. We therefore expect experimentation with offers that yield a small positive gain for the proposer, even if they might be generous to some responders. Early acceptances will begin to reveal who likes which colors.

By round 1, the first trades have revealed partial information: iguana willingly parted with blue for red, suggesting a relatively high red value and lower blue value; zebra accepted blue-for-red, indicating strong interest in blue; cat showed willingness to give up red for blue and also to trade away green for blue, suggesting moderate-to-high blue value and relative indifference about green versus red. With these signals, proposers can refine offers to more directly exploit inferred comparative advantages: iguana as the red-buyer/blue-seller, zebra as the blue-buyer/red-seller, and cat as a flexible intermediary. Still, because beliefs are not precise and the random-choice rule among multiple acceptors adds noise, offers remain somewhat conservative and continue to use green as a balancing chip.

Entering round 2, patterns are clearer. Iguana has repeatedly sacrificed blue to accumulate red, confirming a strong preference for red over blue. Zebra has repeatedly taken blue in exchange for red or blue in exchange for modest concessions, reinforcing their role as a blue-seeker with lower red valuation. Cat has acted as a consistent buyer of blue and relatively willing seller of green and red, suggesting fairly balanced preferences but a particular taste for blue. At this stage, holdings are more skewed: iguana is red-rich and blue-poor, zebra is blue-rich and red-poor, and cat sits in the middle with a healthy blue pile. Final-round offers will try to lock in remaining small gains without creating losses, often involving small numbers of chips to avoid making a mistake given persistent uncertainty about the exact magnitudes of others’ valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on exchanging privately valued red and blue chips under symmetric information about green chips (which are common-value at $0.50). All players start with 10 of each color and know only their own red/blue values. The key surplus opportunities lie in trading chips toward the player who values them more: bear values blue highly ($1.00) and red moderately ($0.70); peacock is relatively low on both blue ($0.40) and red ($0.60); swan is high on blue ($0.90) and low on red ($0.50). This creates strong gains from trade when red moves from bear to swan and blue moves from peacock to bear or swan, but the players must discover these opportunities without knowing others’ exact values. Proposers see their own surplus calculator and will avoid making negative-expected-value offers; responders see their own gain/loss and will only accept positive or at least non-negative offers. Because acceptance is simultaneous and random among acceptors, proposers face uncertainty over which partner (if any) will trade and thus try to design offers that are profitable regardless of which non-sender accepts. Over three short rounds, we expect players to start with conservative, slightly generous offers aimed at at least one partner being strictly better off, gradually inferring others’ preferences from acceptance behavior and adjusting offers. However, limited time and the random-recipient rule mean some trades that would be efficient bilaterally may never occur, and some neutral or mildly inefficient trades may be tried but declined once responders see their own calculators. The forecast below reconstructs a plausible complete game path where players test the waters, learn partial information, and then converge on a few clearly mutually beneficial blue-for-red and blue-for-green/red-for-blue exchanges, while leaving some potential surplus unrealized.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no prior trade history exists, so players mainly probe others’ willingness to trade. Each proposer will choose offers that are strictly profitable given their own values and likely try small quantities to learn who seems to value which colors. Bear, with very high blue value, will try to acquire blue without giving up too much red or green. Peacock, not strongly attached to either red or blue, will test selling blue or red at modest terms, hoping someone else reveals a strong preference. Swan, with high blue and low red, will tentatively move red out in exchange for blue or green. Many early proposals may fail as responders discover that tentative terms are not quite good enough; successful trades this round will typically be clearly positive for both parties and reveal some directional preferences.

By the second round, some information has been revealed: bear has shown strong interest in trading for blue and was willing to pay with both red and green; peacock has shown willingness to give up blue for green, hinting that blue may be relatively low value; swan’s rejected red-for-blue offer suggests that the others do not value blue enough at that steep rate or are wary of moving too much blue. Holdings are now asymmetric, especially between bear and peacock, and players will adjust proposals accordingly. Bear may try to rebalance after gaining green and blue, possibly selling some green or red for blue at more refined terms. Peacock may try to offload remaining blue or pick up red or green at rates that still yield small gains. Swan, having learned that the previous red-heavy offer was unattractive, will likely scale down quantities or offer better terms to make blue acquisition more appealing to at least one partner. Still, uncertainty about exact valuations remains, so players continue experimenting with modest, strictly profitable offers.

In the final round, players understand more about counterpart preferences: blue seems especially valuable to bear, somewhat valuable to swan, and relatively cheap for peacock; green has been used by both bear and peacock as a relatively neutral medium of exchange; red has moved sparsely and appears moderately valued by bear and peacock but less so by swan. With only three proposals left, each proposer will focus on clearly profitable, likely-to-be-accepted trades rather than exploratory ones. Bear, who has accumulated blue and some green, may now be willing to part with a bit of blue at a premium to improve their red or green position. Peacock, with diminished blue holdings, may attempt a small final trade selling red or blue for green or red to eke out minor gains. Swan, having failed to get blue in earlier attempts, might offer a more generous red-for-blue or green-for-blue trade that is just good enough for one partner to accept. Still, given random recipient selection and limited remaining time, some last proposals may be declined if they leave one or both responders with zero or negative surplus on their calculators.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip bargaining game features asymmetric private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green. Each player begins with 10 of each color and privately knows only their own valuations, but all can see each other’s chip holdings and the full trade history. Gorilla and Monkey both value red very highly ($1.00) compared to Camel ($0.30), and Monkey also values blue very highly ($0.90) relative to Gorilla ($0.10) and Camel ($0.60). Green is symmetric at $0.50 to everyone, making it a natural medium of exchange when players are unsure of each other’s valuations.

Gorilla’s main incentives are to acquire red (high value) and perhaps green while offloading low-value blue if anyone will take it. Camel wants to convert some of their relatively low-value red into higher-value blue, and can also use green flexibly because it is worth the same as their average marginal value across colors. Monkey benefits strongly from trading away some green (or even a little red) to gain high-value blue and, secondarily, to gain red from Camel at a discount.

However, none of the players initially know the others’ exact valuations. They must infer them from observed offers and responses over just three rounds. Risk of rejection, plus the rule that any accepting responder is randomly chosen if multiple accept, introduces strategic uncertainty. Proposers need to craft deals that are clearly profitable for at least one counterpart while not being so generous that they give away too much surplus. Typically, early-round offers use green as a relatively safe bargaining chip and are somewhat conservative as players test each other’s willingness to trade. Later rounds may feature more targeted, higher-surplus trades as players update their beliefs: Monkey is likely to be more aggressive in acquiring blue, Camel will try to swap red for blue or green, and Gorilla will seek red while trying to dump blue. Given the short horizon of three rounds, players are likely to prioritize clearly positive, easily calculable gains rather than complex multi-step positioning.

In the forecast below, trades are chosen so that every accepted deal is strictly profitable for both parties (given their private values), while rejected trades are either unprofitable or only marginally positive for at least one potential responder. The trajectory shows early exploratory use of green, followed by more value-targeted trades once inferred preferences become clearer.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players are in the discovery phase: nobody yet knows the others’ valuations, so proposals tend to be simple and use green as a safe medium of exchange. Gorilla, moving first, will likely test whether anyone is willing to sell green cheaply for red. Camel and Monkey will respond based on their own calculators; since green is worth the same to all, they will not accept trades that obviously lose cash value. By the end of the round, at least one accepted trade is likely to reveal hints about who values which colors more, shaping beliefs for later rounds.

By round 1, some structure has emerged. Camel has revealed a willingness to trade away red for blue, and Monkey has shown eagerness to acquire blue and to use green to buy red. Gorilla has learned indirectly that Monkey likely values blue and red quite highly (given their acceptance of Camel’s earlier offer) and will now try to offload low-value blue in exchange for high-value red from Camel. Camel, in turn, begins to suspect that others may value red more than they do, and continues to push red-for-blue conversions. Monkey, now holding more blue, looks for additional chances to upgrade green into higher-value chips while staying cautious about overpaying for red.

In the final round, players understand more about comparative advantages: Gorilla’s attempts to turn blue into red have signaled that red is especially valuable to them; Camel’s repeated red-for-blue trades imply a relatively low valuation of red and a decent valuation of blue; Monkey’s consistent acceptance of blue-acquiring trades reveals that blue is especially valuable. With only one proposal each remaining, players focus on clean, obviously profitable exchanges rather than speculative experiments. Gorilla continues to liquidate blue for red where possible, Camel uses their position to arbitrage between red and blue/green, and Monkey opportunistically picks up more blue if it is cheap enough, while rejecting trades with thin margins or downside risk.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player, multi-round bargaining game with privately known values for red and blue chips and a common known value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color and only knows their own payoff per chip. They observe all trades and holdings over time but must infer others’ valuations from behavior. Because green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone, they serve as a common-value, low-friction currency and natural reference point. Blue and red are both potentially more valuable than green, but how much varies across players: otter values blue the most and red moderately; tiger values red highly and blue almost as much as otter; alligator has very low red value and moderate blue value. This creates strong gains-from-trade: alligator wants to dump red for blue/green, otter especially wants blue but is also happy to move some red if the price is right, and tiger strongly prefers red while also liking blue. The proposal schedule is fixed, so each player gets three proposal opportunities and can update their strategy from earlier responses. Because acceptance decisions are informed by a visible surplus calculator, responders will only accept strictly profitable trades (nonnegative or, empirically, often strictly positive), which disciplines proposers to offer at least a small surplus to any potential recipient. A central tension is that when more than one responder would gain, both may accept and a random recipient is chosen, which makes it costly for proposers to offer very attractive deals that are good for multiple partners when they only really want to trade with one. Over three rounds, early moves are likely exploratory—using green as a numeraire and small quantities to test others’ willingness to pay for red or blue—while later moves refine the exchange rates implied by earlier accept/decline patterns. Players aim to convert their lower-value colors into higher-value ones without accidentally revealing too much about their own valuations or offering overly generous terms that others can exploit in later rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players are symmetric in holdings (10 of each color) but asymmetric in values they privately know. No trade history exists yet, so each player is effectively probing the market. Rational, calculator-assisted behavior implies that proposed trades must leave some surplus for at least one responder, but proposers are uncertain about which color is most valuable to whom. Green’s common value suggests that early trades might use green as a reference: for example, someone with low red value (alligator) can try to sell red for green or blue at a modest rate to see who bites. Since responders will see their own surplus from any posted trade, they will only accept if they strictly gain, and declines will inform proposers about relative valuations. This round sets initial price signals about red versus blue versus green.

Entering round 1, the only executed trade so far is alligator’s sale of 3 RED for 2 BLUE to otter. Publicly, this reveals that otter is willing to part with some blue for red at a 2:3 rate, suggesting otter values red nontrivially, while alligator is comfortable shedding red. However, no one has yet managed to trade green, leaving the green price of red and blue still somewhat ambiguous. Proposers in this round can now condition on observed behavior: tiger sees otter’s interest in red and may try to acquire red from alligator or otter using blue or green; alligator has slightly more blue and fewer red and might continue to dump red, while otter may try again to monetize high blue value or convert between red and green. Still, each proposer faces uncertainty about exact valuations, so they are likely to use modestly improved, but still exploratory, terms to elicit acceptances without being overly generous.

By round 2, several price signals have emerged: alligator has repeatedly sold red, revealing low private value for red; otter has bought red and sold some blue and green, confirming relatively high values for red and especially blue; tiger has bought red with green and accepted a blue-for-green swap with otter, indicating that both red and blue are valuable to tiger but that tiger is willing to use green as a funding source. Holdings are now somewhat shifted: alligator has fewer red, more green and moderate blue; otter has fewer green and blue but more red; tiger has more blue and red financed by green. With only one round left, each player is incentivized to execute any remaining profitable trades quickly, as there is no future to preserve chips for. Uncertainty about valuations is lower: alligator can confidently offer red to tiger or otter for green or blue; otter can try to buy more blue from alligator if the price is favorable; tiger can seek any remaining underpriced red or blue while dumping surplus green. However, random recipient selection still discourages proposals that are highly profitable to more than one responder, so proposers tailor terms to be attractive mainly to their intended partner. This last round often features more targeted and slightly larger trades, as players convert their remaining low-value chips into their high-value ones before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three‑player bargaining game features complementary private values on red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Everyone starts with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to all, so green trades are zero‑sum and only serve to reallocate chips for strategic reasons (e.g., to create more leverage in later trades). The key gains from trade come from moving red chips toward Frog (who values red at $0.90) and away from Dolphin ($0.10) and Pig ($0.40), and moving blue chips toward Frog ($0.50) and Pig ($0.30) and away from Dolphin ($0.10).
