Frog is the strongest holder of red and blue and has the highest private values; they are willing to pay more in green or low‑value red/blue for high‑value red/blue coming from others, but they must avoid overpaying when they do not know that Dolphin’s valuations are so low. Pig is in the middle and can make profitable trades in both directions, especially by selling red to Frog and possibly buying blue from Dolphin at favorable rates. Because proposals are take‑it‑or‑leave‑it with simultaneous responses by the two potential recipients, each proposer must pick a price/quantity pair that is attractive enough for at least one counterparty but not so generous that it leaves surplus on the table. The uncertainty arises from incomplete information about others’ chip values and about their willingness to trade, so early rounds will likely involve conservative, slightly exploitative offers that test the market. Over three rounds (nine total turns), some mutually beneficial trades in red and blue are likely, while most green‑only trades will be avoided unless needed to unlock other deals. Random resolution when both counterparties accept also makes players wary of crafting proposals that are good with one partner but bad with the other. Overall, expect incremental, surplus‑creating trades that shift red and blue toward Frog (and somewhat toward Pig) and green away from them toward Dolphin, with some failed proposals as players probe each other’s reservation values.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players know only their own values and start from symmetric chip holdings. They are likely to test simple, small proposals that seem clearly profitable from their own perspective while being plausibly acceptable to at least one other player. Dolphin, with low values for red and blue, will likely try to sell those colors for green at terms that are comfortably profitable for them but may or may not look attractive to the buyers. Frog, with high red and blue values, will start exploring opportunities to buy red (and possibly blue) from others, probably offering green or some blue at moderate exchange rates. By the second round, players have observed one accepted trade and two rejections. Frog has demonstrated strong demand for red and some willingness to trade blue for it, while one of Frog’s green‑for‑red attempts failed, indicating that terms matter. Pig has opportunistically bought blue from Dolphin and sold red to Frog, profiting from both directions but has not succeeded in acquiring green. With only three proposal turns left, players become more aggressive about locking in remaining surplus: Dolphin will try one more time to convert low‑value chips into green at acceptable terms, Frog will seek a final chunk of red or blue from others, and Pig will try to squeeze out one last arbitrage opportunity without overpaying. Some marginal deals may be rejected if they appear too close to break‑even or require giving up scarce green.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player, three-round bargaining game where each player starts with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. Across the three rounds, the likely pattern is: (1) early small-stakes, information-gathering offers; (2) midgame attempts by Ram and possibly Lion to acquire blue from Octopus (and sometimes from each other) using red or green; and (3) later, slightly larger or more aggressive trades if profitable patterns have been discovered. parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start symmetric in chip holdings (10 of each color) and almost symmetric in values: GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, BLUE is weak at $0.10 for everyone, and the only difference is that unicorn-blue-7096 values RED at $0.50 while badger-green-4722 and peacock-purple-5231 value RED at $0.30. So unicorn strongly wants to accumulate RED, while badger and peacock are more indifferent between RED and GREEN margins but know only their own values. Because values are private, each player sees only that all chip types are within the public range and must infer others’ preferences from proposals and responses. Proposers see an immediate surplus calculator, so they are unlikely to propose offers that are negative in their own terms. Recipients also clearly see whether any posted trade is a gain or loss for them. The main tension is: (1) unicorn is willing to give up GREEN (worth $0.50) or BLUE (worth $0.10) in order to buy RED that is worth $0.50 to them; (2) badger and peacock will generally only sell RED if they get enough GREEN or multiple BLUE chips, but they are unsure how valuable RED is to others; (3) because acceptance is simultaneous and random when multiple players accept, proposers have to design offers that are attractive to at least one counterpart without being so generous that they give away too much expected surplus. Over three short rounds with fixed proposer order, the first round will likely contain exploratory, slightly generous offers trying to map others’ willingness to trade. Later rounds will adjust based on observed acceptances: unicorn will try to scoop more RED cheaply, others may aim for modest gains in GREEN or RED while offloading BLUE. The small horizon and random recipient rule encourage simple, one-step profitable trades instead of complex contingent strategies.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, no one has observed any behavior yet, so proposals are exploratory and relatively conservative. Each player starts from the symmetric 10/10/10 holdings and uses the surplus calculator to ensure self-profitable offers. Badger and peacock, who share the same valuation, will tentatively offer trades that turn low-value BLUE into higher-value GREEN or, if they suspect some players want RED, trade RED for enough GREEN to be slightly better off. Unicorn, with a strong value for RED, is likely to push early offers that exchange GREEN or BLUE for RED in ratios that are clearly profitable for them but might still tempt at least one of the others if those others underweight RED or overweight GREEN. Rejections in this round help each player refine beliefs about how much others value RED versus GREEN.

By the second round, players have observed that trading away pairs of BLUE for a single GREEN can sometimes clear, and that unicorn was willing to pay one GREEN for two RED, hinting that unicorn values RED highly. Badger, having profited from both an early BLUE→GREEN trade and selling RED to unicorn, now suspects that red demand is strong but still only knows their own valuations. Peacock, having just lost a GREEN to badger in round 0, will be more cautious about giving away GREEN cheaply and may instead try to sell RED if they see others hungry for it. Unicorn, encouraged by successfully buying RED, now has better information about badger’s willingness to part with RED for GREEN and might try to repeat or scale up very favorable RED-for-GREEN trades, while recognizing peacock was more hesitant.

Entering the final round, everyone knows the game is ending, so they focus on any remaining small profitable exchanges. Badger has repeatedly traded away RED for GREEN at a tiny gain, and unicorn has consistently snapped up RED when offered at favorable terms, which both badger and peacock can infer from outcomes. This shrinks scope for additional mutually beneficial trades: unicorn is running low on GREEN and BLUE after prior purchases, while badger and peacock are running low on surplus RED. Badger may look to trade any excess BLUE for GREEN, peacock may try one last time to convert BLUE into GREEN or sell a remaining RED if unicorn still has cheap GREEN to pay, and unicorn, if holding some GREEN and BLUE but still seeing RED for sale, might attempt one more slightly favorable RED-for-GREEN or RED-for-BLUE offer. Many marginal offers now barely move value, so players will reject anything that does not give a clear positive net gain on their calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so nobody will ever want to trade GREEN for GREEN, and any GREEN trade is a pure reshuffling of surplus via their differing private values on RED and BLUE. The private values are:
- dog-orange-6603 (Dog): RED=$0.80, BLUE=$1.00, GREEN=$0.50
- mouse-green-4201 (Mouse): RED=$0.20, BLUE=$0.60, GREEN=$0.50
- owl-purple-3001 (Owl): RED=$0.30, BLUE=$1.00, GREEN=$0.50

From these, BLUE is high-value for Dog and Owl, moderate for Mouse. RED is high for Dog but low for Mouse and Owl. GREEN is medium and identical for everyone. The efficient pattern (from an analyst’s perspective) is to shift RED away from Mouse and Owl toward Dog, and to shift BLUE away from Mouse toward Dog and Owl, because Mouse values both colors less. But players don’t know others’ values; each only sees their own, and they infer others’ valuations gradually from proposals and acceptances.

As proposers, each player’s UI shows the net value change for them if their proposed trade is accepted, so they will almost never offer a trade that is negative for themselves. Responders see the same calculator applied to their side once the offer is posted, so they will accept only if it is (weakly) beneficial. Because multiple acceptances lead to a random partner, proposers must design offers that are at least potentially attractive to more than one counterpart or clearly target a specific counterpart via the chip color. However, players don’t know that others value chips as they do, so they will test the waters with conservative, self-favoring trades in early turns, especially in round 0.

Key incentives and tensions:
- Dog highly values both RED and BLUE, especially BLUE. Dog is reluctant to give up BLUE, more willing to pay with GREEN, and may suspect others value GREEN similarly due to its narrow public range. Dog might try to pull in cheap RED from Mouse.
- Mouse has relatively low values for both RED and BLUE (but higher for BLUE than RED). Mouse can often give away RED to others who value it more (Dog) in exchange for GREEN, creating win–win opportunities, but Mouse doesn’t know who values RED highly ex ante.
- Owl values BLUE like Dog ($1.00) but RED is low. Owl will try to convert surplus RED into BLUE or GREEN when profitable, but will be extremely reluctant to part with BLUE at any discount.

Uncertainty: since each player sees only their own chip values, they must infer which colors others want, based on acceptance patterns. In a short 3-round, 9-turn game, there is limited time for learning, so most moves will be plausible, low-risk trades that are obviously good from each player’s own perspective and at least weakly attractive to one counterpart they *guess* might have opposite preferences. The forecast below assumes relatively cautious, near-myopic, but value-aware play: players propose trades with clear gains for themselves and accept only when they see a strictly positive surplus on their calculator, without deep strategic bluffing or long-run signaling.

We forecast a game where (1) Dog and Owl slowly draw some low-valued RED away from Mouse, (2) there are some failed attempts to buy BLUE cheaply that get rejected, and (3) GREEN is often the medium of exchange because its common-value status makes it a natural, symmetric currency. By the end, Dog improves their portfolio most (due to high values on both non-GREEN colors), Mouse improves modestly by offloading RED, and Owl picks up some favorable RED-for-GREEN or RED-for-BLUE adjustments but preserves most of their valuable BLUE.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has 10 of each chip. Nobody has observed any behavior yet, so beliefs about others’ valuations are very diffuse. Players will likely experiment with simple, one-color-for-another swaps that are obviously good for themselves and that they *hope* might also look acceptable to at least one counterpart. Dog moves first and may test whether anyone undervalues GREEN or RED relative to their own high RED and BLUE values. Mouse then may tentatively try exchanging their least-valued color (RED) for something else. Owl, going last, will see what gained traction and adjust their first offer accordingly, while remaining very protective of BLUE.

After round 0, Mouse has successfully converted some low-value RED into GREEN twice (once selling to Dog, once selling to Owl), learning that both counterparts are willing to accept RED-for-GREEN at certain rates. Dog has acquired cheap RED from Mouse and sees that Mouse is happy to part with RED. Owl has similarly converted some RED into GREEN via Mouse. The emerging pattern is that Mouse treats RED as their lowest-value asset, and both Dog and Owl seem comfortable holding more RED if they can pay with GREEN. In round 1, Dog is likely to push this advantage further, possibly trying to get more RED or test whether they can cheaply buy BLUE. Mouse may continue to unload RED, but now understands that they can sometimes get a better rate when multiple parties are interested. Owl, seeing both others’ willingness to move RED and GREEN, may start seeking trades that fine-tune their BLUE and RED holdings while maintaining GREEN as a flexible buffer.

By round 2, a pattern is clear: Mouse repeatedly sheds RED for GREEN, while Dog and Owl accumulate RED using GREEN. A single risky attempt by Owl to buy BLUE using RED was rejected by both Dog and Mouse, revealing that neither is willing to give up BLUE cheaply, even if they personally value it less than Owl does. With only one round left, players will focus on safe, clearly beneficial trades rather than speculative ones. Dog, already holding a lot of high-value RED, may now try a modest final adjustment, such as offloading a bit of GREEN in return for extra RED or testing a small BLUE trade. Mouse, having low-value RED left, will be eager to convert any remaining RED into GREEN or possibly BLUE if a favorable rate appears. Owl will similarly try to trade remaining low-value RED against GREEN or BLUE if possible, but will avoid sacrificing BLUE, which is very valuable to both them and (judging from behavior) to Dog.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game has private values on red and blue chips and common value on green chips, creating classic gains-from-trade opportunities. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it is the natural numeraire/medium of exchange. Red and blue are asymmetric: alligator values red $0.60 and blue $0.50; buffalo values red $0.50 and blue $0.20; rabbit values red $1.00 and blue $0.30. Surplus arises when chips move from players who value them less to those who value them more, but each player only sees their own values and thus must infer others’ values indirectly from observed proposals and responses. Players start with 10 of each chip. Because the interface shows exact personal surplus impacts for any trade, nobody will knowingly accept a trade that lowers their own payoff, and proposals that split gains obviously in favor of the proposer are likely to be rejected unless the responder is nearly indifferent or confused. The big potential gains: (1) red chips should flow toward rabbit, who values red highest ($1.00) and at least as much as anyone else; alligator and buffalo both have lower red values, especially buffalo, creating room for red-for-green or red-for-blue trades benefiting rabbit and the seller; (2) blue chips should flow away from buffalo (who values blue at only $0.20) toward alligator ($0.50) and rabbit ($0.30), with green or red going back to buffalo. However, early in the game, no one knows this structure. The first round is primarily exploratory: proposers test simple one-for-one or slightly skewed ratios and watch who accepts. Rejections reveal that some chips are probably highly valued by a responder; easy acceptances reveal low valuations. Because each round has exactly one proposal by each player, and trades resolve before the next proposal, holdings gradually diverge from the symmetric start, changing both future feasible trades and beliefs. There is also competition risk: when a proposer offers a clearly good deal for some chip (e.g., buying red), multiple responders might want to accept; the proposer cannot choose among them, so offers must anticipate that either potential counterparty might be selected. Over three rounds (nine total proposals), we can expect players to roughly discover the high value of rabbit’s red preference and buffalo’s low blue preference, leading to more profitable trades in later rounds as they respond to prior accept/reject patterns and evolving chip holdings.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players are at symmetric starting holdings (10 of each color) and know nothing about others’ private values. Proposals are likely to be simple, near-even exchanges that test relative appetite for colors. Since everyone values green at $0.50, trading green at a clear disadvantage is unlikely; instead, green will be used cautiously. Early rejections will signal that some chip-color is valued more by a player than the proposer anticipated.

By round 1, one accepted trade has shifted holdings: buffalo has more red and fewer blue, while rabbit has more blue and fewer red. Rabbit’s willingness to give up blue for red in round 0 licenses the inference that rabbit likes red more than blue; buffalo’s willingness to dump blue for red implies blue is relatively weak for buffalo. Alligator saw that both others rejected red-for-green at 1:1, hinting that red might not be dramatically undervalued versus green or that players are cautious. Players now experiment with more directed trades: buffalo offloads surplus red or blue at ratios intended to generate positive surplus for counterparts, and rabbit tries again to buy red in a way that leaves others at least weakly better off.

By round 2, behavior has revealed clearer structure: rabbit has repeatedly tried to buy red, and buffalo has shown willingness to part with blue readily and red at the right ratio. Alligator has benefited from buying blue from buffalo, confirming buffalo’s low blue valuation. The remaining opportunities are mainly to move low-value blue away from buffalo and to channel remaining red toward rabbit, splitting surplus in a way that looks attractive on responders’ calculators. However, holdings are now uneven, and some players may be reluctant to part with green or red given earlier trades. Final-round proposals therefore tend to be somewhat conservative but targeted, aiming for sure small gains rather than speculative large trades that risk rejection.
